segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with upbeat, jazzy music playing over a black screen displaying bold blue text listing news topics like 'TRENDING' and 'SPORTS', before transitioning to a black-and-white scene of a woman walking in the rain under an umbrella, looking at her phone. The scene then shifts to a modern news studio with marble flooring and a blue-lit ceiling, where a male news anchor, dressed in a dark suit and light blue checkered shirt, stands animatedly beside a large screen. He greets viewers, declaring it a "rockin' Friday," and immediately reports that "New York City [is] all shook up" after a "really surprising and rare earthquake" that sent "tremors across the Northeast," noting it was the strongest quake New Jersey has felt in nearly 250 years. As he speaks, the large screen displays 'CITY SHOOK UP' alongside black-and-white surveillance footage of a store shaking, labeled 'New Jersey'. The anchor then transitions to a "new jobs report coming in hot with historic gains," while the screen shows 'HOT REPORT' and a 'HELP WANTED' sign for 'Rose Paving Co.'. He continues, announcing an "NBC News exclusive on the mounting charges coming for Sean Diddy Combs, and now another member of his family as well," as the screen displays 'NEW CHARGES' with an image of Diddy. Finally, he introduces "more royal family drama" with a "new movie about another infamous and explosive BBC interview," coinciding with the screen showing 'PRINCE 'SCOOP'' and a Netflix documentary clip. Throughout his animated delivery, the NBC News Now logo is visible, and the upbeat music returns as the segment concludes.
segments.visual_original_caption: The video opens with a black screen displaying bold, blue text listing topics such as 'TRENDING', 'SPORTS', 'WORLD', 'CLIMATE', 'CRIME', and 'CULTURE'. It transitions to a black-and-white scene of a woman walking in the rain under an umbrella, looking at her phone, with a building in the background. The scene shifts to a news studio where a male news anchor, dressed in a dark suit and light blue checkered shirt, stands beside a large screen. He gestures toward the screen as it cycles through various headlines and visuals: 'CITY SHOOK UP' with surveillance footage of a store shaking during an earthquake, 'HOT REPORT' with a 'HELP WANTED' sign for a paving company, 'NEW CHARGES' with an image of Diddy, and 'PRINCE 'SCOOP'' with a Netflix documentary clip. The studio is modern, with a red table, marble flooring, and a blue-lit ceiling. The anchor holds a piece of paper and speaks animatedly, pointing at the screen to emphasize each segment. The NBC News Now logo is visible in the corner throughout the studio scenes.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment begins with Male News Anchor, Tom Costello, in a bright studio, standing before a Large Screen Display that initially shows a dark, silhouetted scene from the Prince 'Scoop' Clip, labeled 'NETFLIX' and 'PRINCE 'SCOOP'', as he discusses a member of the Royal Family defending his relationship with Jeffrey Epstein. The screen then transitions to a blue background listing various news topics, including 'THE ORIGINAL FINAL FOUR', prompting Costello to ask if viewers are watching the Women's Final Four games tonight, predicting a "record-breaking night" for women's college basketball. The display shifts to a black-and-white montage under the headline 'RECORD-BREAKING NIGHT?', showing a female basketball player in action, with 'ESPN' and 'LOUISVILLE' visible, as Costello mentions a live team covering the event later in the show. Costello then returns, standing beside a screen displaying 'Hallie Jackson NOW_', and explains he is "in for Hallie," before transitioning to "concerns about safety" following a "shocking and rare earthquake" that "rocked parts of the East Coast," noting it was the strongest quake the New Jersey epicenter has seen in nearly 250 years. Surveillance Footage from a home in Middlesex, NJ, then shows a hallway violently shaking with loud banging sounds and a person exclaiming "What happened?!", overlaid with a 'BREAKING NEWS_' banner stating '4.8-MAGNITUDE EARTHQUAKE RUMBLES PARTS OF THE NORTHEAST'. Costello reappears, now standing before a Google Earth map highlighting the earthquake's epicenter in Lebanon, NJ, confirming it was a 4.8 magnitude quake centered about 50 miles outside of New York, with the studio maintaining a bright, professional ambiance and blue accents.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a Male News Anchor, speaking in a professional, urgent tone, standing in a modern Studio Environment with marble flooring and a blue-lit ceiling. He presents breaking news about a 4.8-magnitude earthquake that struck Lebanon, NJ, on Friday, April 5, 2024, at 10:23 AM EDT, noting that people felt the shakes from Philadelphia up to Boston. Behind him, the Large Screen Display shows an Earthquake Map Display with a red target over Lebanon, NJ, indicating the epicenter, magnitude, and timestamp. The anchor gestures toward the screen as he explains that in one New Jersey town, over a dozen people were evacuated due to structural damage, with buildings 'leaning in.' The screen then transitions to Surveillance Footage from Newark, NJ, showing houses with visible structural damage, including a collapsed roof and broken siding, reinforcing the physical effects. Next, the Large Screen Display switches to black-and-white Surveillance Footage from 'Boonton Coffee' in New Jersey, where shelves and tables shake violently during the quake, and patrons react with surprise and movement, though no specific sounds from the footage are heard. The anchor continues to narrate, stating that teams are checking bridges, roads, buildings, and tunnels across the state, many of which are old, and that there are no reports of injuries yet. He then explains that the earthquake triggered ground stops at two major airports, diverting several flights. The screen displays an FAA Tweet warning of potential impacts on air traffic facilities in New York, New Jersey, Philadelphia, and Baltimore. The anchor introduces a Delta pilot, and the segment cuts to a blue graphic overlay with a quote from a Delta pilot on a flight into LaGuardia Airport. The pilot's slightly muffled voice is heard stating that while they received earthquake alerts, they have not been affected, and the weather is fine, though the ground may be shaky. The anchor returns, now standing before Aerial Residential Damage Footage showing emergency vehicles and fire trucks responding to damage, reinforcing the earthquake’s real-world consequences, and humorously remarks about East Coasters being perceived as 'wimps' before the segment ends.
segments.visual_original_caption: The segment opens with the Male News Anchor standing in the Studio Environment, presenting breaking news about a 4.8-magnitude earthquake that struck Lebanon, NJ, on Friday, April 5, 2024, at 10:23 AM EDT. The anchor gestures toward the screen while holding a paper, emphasizing the event’s impact. The screen then transitions to surveillance footage from Newark, NJ, showing a row of houses with visible structural damage, including a collapsed roof and broken siding, indicating the earthquake’s physical effects. Next, the Large Screen Display switches to black-and-white Surveillance Footage from a coffee shop in New Jersey, labeled 'Boonton Coffee,' where shelves and tables shake violently during the quake, and patrons react with surprise and movement. The anchor continues to narrate, pointing to the footage. The screen then displays a tweet from The FAA, warning of potential impacts on air traffic facilities in New York, New Jersey, Philadelphia, and Baltimore, with operations resuming as quickly as possible. The anchor gestures toward the tweet, explaining its significance. The segment cuts to a blue graphic overlay with a quote from a Delta pilot on a flight into LaGuardia Airport, stating that while they received earthquake alerts, they have not been affected and the weather is fine, though the ground may be shaky. The anchor returns, now standing before an aerial view of a residential area with emergency vehicles and fire trucks responding to damage, reinforcing the earthquake’s real-world consequences. Throughout, the Studio Environment remains consistent with its modern design, marble flooring, red table, and blue-lit ceiling, while the anchor maintains a professional demeanor, using hand gestures to emphasize key points.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a Male News Anchor in a modern studio, gesturing while holding a paper in front of a Large Screen Display showing an aerial view of a residential area with emergency vehicles, as a red 'BREAKING NEWS' banner reads '4.8-MAGNITUDE EARTHQUAKE RUMBLES PARTS OF THE NORTHEAST, PROMPTING CONCERNS OF BUILDING SAFETY.' He speaks with a concerned and authoritative tone, emphasizing that "this isn't California" and that it's "so scary for so many people because the East Coast is not known for this kind of thing," highlighting that "many buildings really aren't built for earthquakes here," raising "a lot of concern and questions tonight about structural safety." He then introduces meteorologist Michelle Grossman before transitioning to "my friend Rehema Ellis who's on the ground in Jersey." The scene shifts to a split-screen, showing Rehema Ellis live from Lebanon, NJ, at 5:03 PM, wearing a navy blue coat, purple scarf, and glasses on a street with houses and passing cars, while on the right, a female anchor stands beside an Earthquake Map Display highlighting the epicenter in New Jersey and noting shaking felt from DC to Boston. The Male News Anchor asks Rehema, "my daughter lives in New York City, I got an urgent text message from her this morning, rattled, she was startled," and questions, "this doesn't happen very often on the East Coast, right? What are you hearing from folks there in Jersey?" The camera then focuses solely on Rehema Ellis, with the 'BREAKING NEWS' banner and the updated 'LEBANON, NJ 5:04 PM' timestamp visible. She speaks directly to the camera, confirming, "You are right, it does not happen often," and states that "the folks here in New Jersey, they were as you said, rattled by all of all of this, leaving them to wonder what in the heck is going on." She then prepares to introduce a local resident, saying, "I want you to hear from a woman who's just down the street from where I'm standing right now. She and her family were at home, listen to what they said about how they felt this earthquake hitting them," as the background shows a quiet suburban street under overcast skies.

The scene transitions to a split-screen format: on the left, a female correspondent, Rehema Ellis, appears live from Lebanon, NJ, wearing a navy blue coat, purple scarf, and glasses, standing on a street with houses and passing cars in the background; on the right, a female anchor in a pink blouse stands beside the Earthquake Map Display, which highlights the epicenter in New Jersey and notes shaking felt from DC to Boston. The time stamp in the top left corner reads 'LEBANON, NJ 5:03 PM,' updating to 5:04 PM. The background shows a quiet suburban street with light traffic and residential homes, under overcast skies, emphasizing the real-time reporting from the affected area.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a red NBC News Now graphic before transitioning to an indoor scene where Jenielle Jensen, a resident of Lebanon, NJ, dressed in a pink sweatshirt, animatedly recounts her earthquake experience to two young girls in a well-lit living room featuring a fireplace, large TV, round mirror, and potted plants. Jensen's voice is clear and expressive as she describes the event as "absolutely unbelievable," stating she thought "our house coming down" and that "everybody was screaming" because the "walls were trembling." The scene then cuts to NBC News correspondent Rehema Ellis, standing outdoors on a suburban street in Lebanon, NJ, at 5:04 PM, wearing a navy blue coat, purple scarf, and glasses, reporting live with cars passing behind her against a backdrop of residential houses and a crosswalk. The lower third displays the breaking news headline: '4.8-MAGNITUDE EARTHQUAKE RUMBLES PARTS OF THE NORTHEAST, PROMPTING CONCERNS OF BUILDING SAFETY.' Rehema Ellis, speaking with a clear, authoritative voice, references Jensen's account, noting it felt like "something coming up from the ground that was rattling," and shares another resident's experience of feeling like "a plane roaring right over her house," though thankfully "nothing was broken" and there were "no structural damage." She confirms that town officials report "no reports of any damages, any injuries, of any major sort." The segment concludes with a split-screen view: on the left, a Male News Anchor, identified as Tom, in a dark suit and light blue checkered shirt, is seated in a modern studio with blue lighting and multiple screens displaying 'Hallie Jackson NOW'; on the right, Rehema Ellis continues her report outdoors under overcast weather with light traffic, as Tom begins to speak, saying, "You know, the first time I felt..."

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a split-screen view, featuring a Male News Anchor in a dark suit and light blue checkered shirt, seated in the Studio Environment with the 'Hallie Jackson NOW' screen behind him, on the left. On the right, NBC News correspondent Rehema Ellis, wearing a navy blue coat, purple scarf, and glasses, reports live from Lebanon, NJ, with a suburban street and passing cars in the background, and a 'LEBANON, NJ 5:05 PM' timestamp. The Male News Anchor's voice is heard describing his personal experience of the earthquake feeling like a 'freight train' and questioning the 30-minute delay in alerts. Rehema Ellis responds, her voice detailing her own experience as a New York City resident, where she felt her kitchen move while watching MSNBC, and received an alert about 20 minutes later, before introducing a soundbite from officials regarding the delayed warnings. Throughout this, a 'Breaking News' lower third remains constant, stating '4.8-MAGNITUDE EARTHQUAKE RUMBLES PARTS OF THE NORTHEAST, PROMPTING CONCERNS OF BUILDING SAFETY'. The scene then transitions to a press briefing where Zachary Iscol, Commissioner of New York City Emergency Management, stands at a podium with the NYC seal, flanked by officials and a Sign Language Interpreter. Behind him, an American flag and multiple screens display maps and data, including an Earthquake Map Display showing the epicenter in Lebanon, NJ, and a time '19:21'. Zachary Iscol's voice is heard addressing the alert delay, stating that '20 minutes time to target is pretty fast for public notification' and explaining the necessary confirmation process with USGS.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with Zachary Iscol, Commissioner of New York City Emergency Management, speaking at a podium adorned with the NYC seal, flanked by officials and a Sign Language Interpreter actively signing. He states that there are "a lot of things that can cause buildings to shake" and emphasizes the need to "put out the proper guidance." The formal Press Briefing Background features an American flag and screens displaying data, while a Breaking News Lower Third announces a "4.8-MAGNITUDE EARTHQUAKE RUMBLES PARTS OF THE NORTHEAST, PROMPTING CONCERNS OF BUILDING SAFETY." A distinct chime signals a transition to NBC News correspondent Rehema Ellis, reporting live from Lebanon, NJ, at 5:06 PM. Dressed in a navy blue coat, purple scarf, and glasses, she stands on a suburban street with passing cars, explaining that "City officials" will have "building inspectors available this weekend for anyone who might feel some concerns about their property." The broadcast then cuts to a split-screen, showing a Male News Anchor in the Studio Environment, holding glasses, who thanks Rehema and introduces meteorologist Michelle Grossman, asking about aftershocks and how this event compares to the 2011 earthquake, noting "significant damage to the Washington Monument, to the National Cathedral, cracks up and down the facades." The Large Screen Display behind them shows an Earthquake Map Display with the epicenter in New Jersey, labeled '4.8 MAGNITUDE EARTHQUAKE' and 'SHAKING FELT FROM DC TO BOSTON'. Michelle Grossman, in a pink blouse and beige pants, gestures as she recounts her experience in 2011, stating, "that was the first earthquake that I felt," and describes the sensation as "that sliding. It almost feels like the the earth is just moving under you, not so much the shaking." Archival footage from 2011, showing workers on scaffolding repairing a historic building, visually reinforces the discussion of past earthquake impacts.
segments.visual_original_caption: The video segment begins with a press briefing led by Zachary Iscol, Commissioner of New York City Emergency Management, standing at a podium with the NYC seal, flanked by officials and a Sign Language Interpreter actively signing. The setting is a formal Press Briefing Background with an American flag and multiple screens displaying maps and data. The Breaking News Lower Third announces a 4.8-magnitude earthquake rattling the Northeast, prompting building safety concerns. The scene transitions to Rehema Ellis, an NBC News correspondent, reporting live from Lebanon, NJ, at 5:06 PM, wearing a navy blue coat, purple scarf, and glasses, standing on a suburban street with passing cars and houses in the background. The broadcast then cuts to a split-screen format: the Male News Anchor in the Studio Environment, holding glasses and speaking, while Rehema Ellis continues her report. The Large Screen Display then shows an Earthquake Map Display with the epicenter in New Jersey, labeled '4.8 MAGNITUDE EARTHQUAKE' and 'SHAKING FELT FROM DC TO BOSTON', with a female meteorologist, Michelle Grossman, standing beside it in a pink blouse and beige pants, gesturing as she explains the event. The segment includes archival footage from 2011, showing workers on scaffolding repairing a historic building, likely referencing past earthquake damage. The narrative emphasizes the earthquake’s wide impact and historical context, with consistent breaking news banners and studio visuals reinforcing the urgency and scale of the event.

segments.integrated_caption: NBC News meteorologist Michelle Grossman, dressed in a light pink blouse and beige pants, stands in a modern, blue-lit news studio, actively gesturing with her right hand while holding a remote in her left. She speaks clearly and authoritatively, explaining that a previous earthquake was 5.8 magnitude, "10 times more powerful," and that aftershocks have occurred "pretty much every hour since that 10 o'clock earthquake." Behind her, a large screen displays a topographical map titled 'EARTH SHAKING MORNING | FRIDAY MORNING', highlighting the 4.8-magnitude earthquake's epicenter in New Jersey with concentric green rings, and text stating 'SHAKING FELT FROM DC TO BOSTON'. Michelle continues, detailing the USGS prediction of aftershocks over the next "day or two," including a "4% chance of feeling a 5.0 or greater" and a "48% chance of feeling a 3.0 magnitude or less." As she points to the screen, it transitions to a broader 'EARTHQUAKE RANGE' map showing a 300-mile radius from the epicenter, encompassing cities like New York, Boston, and Washington. She specifies the epicenter was "3 miles from Lebanon" and "4 miles from White House Station," emphasizing the 4.8-magnitude event as "notable" and "historical," felt "300 miles from the epicenter" to "portions of New England." The screen then displays a graphic stating 'NEW JERSEY EARTHQUAKES: 3RD STRONGEST IN 280 YEARS OF EARTHQUAKE HISTORY MAGNITUDE 4.8' with a cracked earth background, reinforcing the historical context she provides. Throughout the segment, a red 'BREAKING NEWS_' banner at the bottom reads '4.8-MAGNITUDE EARTHQUAKE RATTLES THE NORTHEAST WITH BIGGEST EARTHQUAKE IN OVER A DECADE'.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with NBC News meteorologist Michelle Grossman, in a light pink blouse and beige pants, standing before a large screen displaying New Jersey earthquake data. She gestures towards the screen, explaining that the 4.8-magnitude earthquake is the third strongest in 280 years, then highlights the 5.2 magnitude quake in 1737 as the second strongest, and the 5.3 magnitude quake in 1783 as the strongest, noting that many have not felt such an event in their lifetime and that aftershocks are possible. The scene transitions to a split-screen view, showing a Male News Anchor in a dark suit and light blue checkered shirt in the Studio Environment, with a 'Hallie Jackson NOW' screen behind him, as he thanks Michelle and acknowledges the lack of significant damage or injuries. A wide shot of the studio then reveals a Large Screen Display cycling through headlines like 'HOSTAGE', 'SAFETY CONCERNS', 'NEW YORK CITY', 'TAIWAN', 'BIDEN', 'ISRAEL', and 'MARKETS', before shifting to a 'RACE TO RESCUE' graphic with an aerial view of a mountainous, snow-covered landscape. The Male News Anchor then narrates over raw, shaky surveillance-style video from the Pingtung Fire Department, showing rescue teams in helmets and yellow gear navigating a debris-filled, rocky mountain pass amidst dust and smoke. On-screen text reads 'RESCUE TEAMS COMB THROUGH MOUNTAINS IN TAIWAN TO FIND TRAPPED QUAKE VICTIMS', credited to the Taiwan National Fire Agency, as the anchor describes rescuers facing landslides and falling rocks, mentioning a rescuer giving an injured man a piggyback ride out of a cave and the discovery of two more bodies in the mountains.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a split-screen displaying the aftermath of Taiwan's 7.4-magnitude earthquake as of April 5, while a male anchor's voice reports the grim statistics: "It's now to 12, the number of people killed in the 7.4 magnitude quake, more than 1,100 injured, 600 plus trapped, at least 13 are missing." On the left, TVBS footage shows a severely tilted multi-story building in an urban area with debris and bystanders, while on the right, rescue personnel from the Taiwan Fire Department, in yellow and red uniforms, navigate rubble inside a collapsed structure searching for survivors. After a brief transition sound, the scene shifts to a nighttime report from Hualien, Taiwan, where NBC News International Correspondent Janis Mackey Frayer, with short blonde hair and wearing a dark button-up shirt, stands before a damaged building illuminated by emergency lights. She reports, "We were inside Taroko Gorge National Park today, where for days rescue teams have been working to reach hundreds of people stranded in the mountains. They've been cut off by landslides. Helicopters have been taking supplies to them, things like food and water to tie them over until they can be airlifted out." As she speaks, the video cuts to daytime footage from the Fire Bureau of Taichung City Government, showing rescue teams in high-visibility vests trekking through a mountainous, debris-laden landscape, carrying a stretcher with a victim, followed by a scene at a helipad where Taiwan National Fire Agency personnel load a victim into a red helicopter marked 'NA-713'. Janis continues, explaining, "Most of them have been trapped at a hotel inside the park where they're said to be safe, but there are still several people who have been reported missing. Time is of the essence. That 72-hour window that rescue officials talk about maximizing, it's now beginning to close. Rain has come into the forecast, and the main challenge remains aftershocks." The segment then shows close-ups of medical personnel treating an injured man on a stretcher, attended by a police officer and rescue workers, before returning to the urban devastation with dramatic views of a leaning building and a collapsed structure. Throughout, the lower third displays 'RESCUE TEAMS COMB THROUGH MOUNTAINS IN TAIWAN TO FIND TRAPPED QUAKE VICTIMS' and the NBC News Now logo.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with dramatic TVBS footage of a severely tilted building in an urban area, surrounded by debris and observed by bystanders under daylight, illustrating the aftermath of a powerful earthquake in Taiwan. NBC News International Correspondent Janis Mackey Frayer then reports live from Hualien, Taiwan, at night, standing before damaged buildings and emergency response activity. Wearing a dark button-up shirt, with short blonde hair, she gestures expressively as she details the ongoing rescue efforts, noting that '400 of them now and counting' are trapped, and explaining that rescue teams must retreat during alerts. The lower third displays the 'NBC News Now' logo and the headline 'RESCUE TEAMS COMB THROUGH MOUNTAINS IN TAIWAN TO FIND TRAPPED QUAKE VICTIMS'. Janis highlights Taiwan's advanced earthquake preparedness, including cell phone alerts, public awareness, and strict building codes that have led to structures being reinforced with steel beams. The report intercuts with surveillance video from 'cti 中天新聞' showing a restaurant's ceiling and interior shaking violently, with tables and chairs moving and people reacting. This is followed by footage from the Ma Cherie Maternity Centre, timestamped 24/04/03 07:58:59, where staff in pink uniforms and masks tend to newborns in incubators as the room shakes, a red '平安' (peace/safety) decoration visible on the wall. Rescue operations are then shown, with a red fire truck and crane lifting a basket of rescuers towards a damaged building, followed by a close-up of the basket ascending the side of a leaning structure. Returning to Janis Mackey Frayer, she continues her report, pointing to the 'tilting building behind me' and revealing that after days on the verge of collapse, officials have decided to demolish it. The segment concludes with a transition to the modern NBC News Now studio, featuring marble flooring and blue-lit ceilings, where a large blue screen displays topics like 'TAIWAN', 'BIDEN', 'ISRAEL', and 'ECLIPSE', before showing a black-and-white image of a collapsed bridge with 'PROMISING MORE AID' overlaid. A studio host, whose voice is heard, thanks Janis and remarks on the 'interesting' fact that the nurses ran 'to the babies, rather than running away,' referencing the earlier maternity center footage, with a screen labeled 'Hallie Jackson NOW' visible in the background.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens in a modern news studio, where a Male News Anchor, speaking with a professional tone, introduces a report on federal aid for Baltimore. Behind him, a Large Screen Display shows a black-and-white image of a collapsed bridge with 'PROMISING MORE AID' and 'Petty Officer 2nd Class Taylor Bacon' overlaid. The scene transitions to President Biden, wearing a blue puffer jacket and black cap, speaking with a distinct, somewhat gravelly voice at a podium in Baltimore, flanked by officials. He declares, "Your nation has your back," followed by applause, while on-screen text reads 'PRESIDENT BIDEN TELLS BALTIMORE: 'THE NATION HAS YOUR BACK'' and 'BRIDGE COLLAPSE'. Aerial footage then reveals a massive container ship, marked 'MAERSK', having collided with and partially destroyed a large steel truss bridge, with debris scattered in the water. Returning to the studio, the Male News Anchor continues to narrate over a Large Screen Display showing the 'Impacts of Deadly Bridge Collapse Graphic', listing 'Six people killed', 'Massive travel disruption', 'Thousands of jobs impacted', and 'Global trade disrupted'. The segment concludes with a split-screen view, featuring the Male News Anchor in the blue-lit studio on the left, and a Correspondent in a dark coat standing outdoors in Washington DC on the right, as the anchor discusses the ongoing efforts and global trade implications, asking the correspondent to elaborate.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a split-screen view, featuring a Male News Anchor in a dark suit in a studio on the left, and Correspondent George Solis outdoors in Baltimore on the right, wearing a dark coat. A lower third displays 'PRESIDENT BIDEN TELLS BALTIMORE: 'THE NATION HAS YOUR BACK'' and 'BRIDGE COLLAPSE'. The Male News Anchor briefly sets the context, mentioning that the victims' families are from Mexico or Central America, before George Solis takes over, addressing 'Tom'. Solis describes President Biden's role as 'consoler in chief', recounting an anecdote shared by Biden about a text message from one of the workers to his girlfriend, sent just before the collapse, stating they had 'just finished pouring the cement' and were 'waiting for it to dry'. The scene then transitions to a full-screen graphic titled 'Baltimore Bridge Collapse Victims_', displaying six portraits, including Alejandro Hernandez Fuentes and Dorlian Castillo Cabrera (bodies recovered), Miguel Luna, José López, Maynor Suazo, and a silhouette for an unidentified victim, set against a blue bokeh background. The video cuts back to George Solis in Baltimore, who continues reporting, gesturing as he describes a growing memorial and vigil behind him, where community members, family, and friends are paying respects to the six men who lost their lives, noting four are still unaccounted for. He briefly mentions having spoken with Julio Cervantes, one of the bridge survivors. The segment concludes with dramatic aerial footage of a large MAERSK container ship colliding with a steel truss bridge, showing significant structural damage and debris in the water, with the same lower third text overlay.

The video transitions to Correspondent George Solis, standing outdoors in Baltimore, wearing a dark coat, reporting on a person who, despite a chest injury, is now in better spirits but still seeking privacy while mourning the loss of family members. Solis emphasizes that the pain is "still all too real" for those families and highlights President Biden's visit to tour the wreckage and speak with them, understanding their grief. A lower third graphic updates to 'NEW: PRESIDENT BIDEN TOURS SITE OF DEADLY BRIDGE COLLAPSE IN BALTIMORE'. segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with stark footage from Deir Al-Balah, Gaza, showing a severely damaged white pickup truck labeled 'World Central Kitchen' amidst debris, with palm trees and a damaged wall in the background. A lower third graphic states, 'IDF DISMISSES TWO SENIOR OFFICERS OVER KILLING OF SEVEN AID WORKERS IN GAZA'. The Male News Anchor's voice immediately provides context, discussing demands for an independent probe into the strikes, which Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu called 'unintentional,' and the Biden administration's 'cautious optimism' over Israel's new steps to allow more aid into Gaza, following a US ultimatum to Netanyahu to protect civilians or risk American support. As the audio continues, the scene transitions to a bustling, war-torn street in Gaza, where people navigate through rubble, some carrying goods, while others gather around makeshift stalls with colorful umbrellas and large cooking pots, highlighting the humanitarian crisis. The segment then shifts to a split-screen news studio format, where the Male News Anchor, seated in a modern studio with a 'Hallie Jackson NOW' screen behind him, introduces NBC News International Correspondent Hala Gorani. Hala appears live from Jerusalem at night, standing before a stone wall with city lights blurred in the background, identified by a lower third as 'HALA GORANI, NBC NEWS INTERNATIONAL CORRESPONDENT, JERUSALEM 12:14 AM'. The anchor asks Hala to elaborate on the Israeli military's 'mea culpa' and the removal of officers, questioning its significance. Hala responds, confirming that 'two officers were fired and that some military commanders were reprimanded,' but stresses that 'it doesn't appear as though any charges will be brought against them for what the Israeli military itself has called a grave error.' The lower third updates to '12:15 AM' as the serious discussion continues.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with NBC News International Correspondent Hala Gorani reporting live from Jerusalem at 12:15 AM, standing outdoors in a beige trench coat against a blurred stone wall and city lights. The lower third displays the headline: 'IDF DISMISSES TWO SENIOR OFFICERS OVER KILLING OF SEVEN AID WORKERS IN GAZA' under the 'ISRAEL-HAMAS WAR' banner. Hala Gorani's clear, professional voice explains the IDF's findings, stating there was a "case of mistaken identity" leading to "three strikes against that World Central Kitchen convoy." She notes the reprimand of senior commanders and the dismissal of two middle-ranking officers, but highlights that "it doesn't appear as though beyond this reprimand... there will be any consequences" at this stage for what the Israeli military has acknowledged was "a mistake and a case of mistaken identity," calling it a "tragedy for the people on the ground." As she speaks, the scene cuts to a graphic overlay on a destroyed vehicle, quoting the IDF's admission of "serious violation of the commands... a grave mistake stemming from a serious failure due to a mistaken identification, errors in decision-making..." The visuals then transition to Deir Al-Balah, Gaza, showing the aftermath: a burnt-out white pickup truck, clearly labeled 'World Central Kitchen', with visible damage and debris, as people walk nearby. The location tag 'TUESDAY DEIR AL-BALAH, GAZA' appears in the top left corner, while the same headline remains at the bottom. Gorani continues, emphasizing that "this is not an isolated event," citing the UN Secretary-General and revealing that "almost 200 aid workers have been killed since October 7th," concluding that this is a "wider problem that goes beyond the tragedy that left seven World Central Kitchen workers dead."

The scene transitions to NBC News International Correspondent Hala Gorani, reporting live from Jerusalem at night in a beige trench coat, whose voice is heard discussing the wider issue of how the Israeli military is conducting its operation, which has led to the deaths of tens of thousands of Palestinians and the destruction of infrastructure in Gaza. A split-screen interview then shows a Male News Anchor in a dark suit, gesturing in a studio with 'Hallie Jackson NOW' visible, asking Hala Gorani if President Biden's ultimatum to Netanyahu has resonated with the Israeli government. Hala Gorani responds, explaining that Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu has opened the Erez Crossing, a significant development in northern Gaza that had been closed since October 7th. She adds that U.S. Secretary of State Antony Blinken, like many others, is urging a 'wait and see' approach regarding Israel's promises, as a graphic titled 'NEW STEPS ISRAEL SAYS IT WILL TAKE TO LET MORE AID INTO GAZA' lists the temporary reopening of the Erez Crossing, allowing sea aid, and increasing aid shipments from Jordan. The segment concludes with a brief clip of Antony Blinken, identified by a lower third as 'U.S. SECRETARY OF STATE GIVES NETANYAHU ULTIMATUM', speaking from Brussels in front of a U.S. government aircraft, though his statement is cut short.

audio_updated_entities.Male News Anchor: A man in a dark suit and light blue checkered shirt, seated in the Studio Environment, with a screen behind him displaying 'Hallie Jackson NOW', gesturing as he speaks during a live segment. He acknowledges and thanks Hala Gorani, then reports on the stock market closing 'in the green' after a 'stronger than expected' March jobs report, describing it as 'booming' and 'eye-popping,' while noting the Dow's 'worst week this year.'

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of a 'HELP WANTED' sign for Carvel Ice Cream, followed by similar signs, including one for Rose Paving Co. seeking an Asphalt Luteman and Pavebox Operator, all displayed under the recurring 'U.S. ADDS 303,000 JOBS IN MARCH, IN STRONGER-THAN-EXPECTED REPORT' lower third and an 'INFLATION IN AMERICA' tag. A Male News Anchor's voice is heard, noting the S&P and Nasdaq are up over 1%, before highlighting that the economy added a 'stunning' 303,000 jobs in March, 50% more than the predicted 200,000, marking the 39th straight month of job growth. The visuals transition to a retail environment, showing a customer inserting a credit card and a cashier handling cash, as the anchor's voice continues, emphasizing this positive job report paints a 'very different picture' from a year ago when a recession was anticipated. The scene then shifts to the Male News Anchor in a modern studio, gesturing as he introduces NBC News Senior Business Correspondent Christine Romans. A split-screen appears, showing Christine Romans, identified by a lower third, standing beside a large screen displaying a 'Jobs Gained Per Month' bar graph from the Bureau of Labor Statistics. The Male News Anchor asks her about the implications of this 'good news' for interest rates, suggesting a 'hot' economy might keep prices high. Christine Romans, gesturing towards the graph, responds by questioning whether interest rates should be cut when the job market looks so strong.

segments.integrated_caption: NBC News Senior Business Correspondent Christine Romans, standing in a modern studio with blue-lit accents, gestures toward a bar graph titled 'Jobs Gained Per Month' as she explains that the economy "does not look like it needs a little bit of help here," noting how the job market "has actually accelerated." The graph behind her, sourced from the Bureau of Labor Statistics, highlights 303,000 jobs added in March, stronger than expected, while a lower third banner confirms 'U.S. ADDS 303,000 JOBS IN MARCH, IN STRONGER-THAN-EXPECTED REPORT' alongside an 'INFLATION IN AMERICA' tag. Transitioning, she points to a line graph showing the 'Unemployment Rate' from 2010 to 2024, highlighting the 3.8% mark for March, stating, "the unemployment rate, you talked about all those superlatives, a soup of superlatives. Here's one, 40 or 28 months below 4% for the jobless rate. That hasn't happened since the 1960s." The lower third updates to 'UNEMPLOYMENT RATE FALLS TO 3.8 PERCENT IN MARCH, FROM 3.9 PERCENT IN FEBRUARY.' As the scene splits to include a Male News Anchor listening in a separate studio environment, Christine continues her professional and informative report, discussing how "six months ago... the feeling was there'd be three rate cuts by the Fed this year," but now, "what I see here is a job market that has absorbed all those rate hikes and is still strong." She adds that "wages, 4.1%... a little bit cooler than the month before," suggesting this "gives some comfort to the Fed that it can just wait. It doesn't have to be cutting interest rates here anytime soon," concluding that "you still have wages rising faster than your bills."
segments.visual_original_caption: The segment opens with Christine Romans, NBC News Senior Business Correspondent, standing in a modern studio environment with blue-lit accents and a large screen behind her. She is dressed in a black blazer over a white top, accessorized with a necklace and bracelets, and gestures toward a bar graph titled 'Jobs Gained Per Month' sourced from the Bureau of Labor Statistics. The graph displays monthly job gains in thousands from March to February, with the March bar highlighted in red, indicating 303,000 jobs added—stronger than expected. The lower third banner reads 'U.S. ADDS 303,000 JOBS IN MARCH, IN STRONGER-THAN-EXPECTED REPORT' alongside the 'INFLATION IN AMERICA' tag. As she transitions, the screen changes to a line graph titled 'Unemployment Rate,' showing the rate from 2010 to 2024, with a sharp spike in 2020 and a steady decline since. She points to the 3.8% mark for March, and the lower third updates to 'UNEMPLOYMENT RATE FALLS TO 3.8 PERCENT IN MARCH, FROM 3.9 PERCENT IN FEBRUARY.' The scene then splits, introducing the Male News Anchor in the Studio Environment, seated and wearing a dark suit with a light blue checkered shirt, with a screen behind him displaying 'Hallie Jackson NOW.' He listens and responds as Christine continues her report, maintaining a professional and informative tone throughout.

segments.integrated_caption: The video segment opens with a split-screen view, featuring a Male News Anchor, dressed in a dark suit and light blue checkered shirt, seated in a blue-lit studio environment on the left, gesturing while speaking with a 'Hallie Jackson NOW' screen behind him. On the right, Christine Romans, the NBC News Senior Business Correspondent, stands beside a large screen displaying an 'Unemployment Rate' graph, a line chart from 2010 to 2024 showing a sharp spike in 2020 and a decline to 3.8% in March, sourced from the Bureau of Labor Statistics. A lower third banner reads 'UNEMPLOYMENT RATE FALLS TO 3.8 PERCENT IN MARCH, FROM 3.9 PERCENT IN FEBRUARY', accompanied by an 'INFLATION IN AMERICA' tag and the NBC News Now logo. The Male News Anchor's voice is heard noting that while wages are up 4%, grocery items have increased by 30% over two years, indicating that wage growth isn't keeping pace with inflation. The focus then shifts entirely to Christine Romans, wearing a black blazer over a white top with a necklace and bracelets, her blonde hair styled shoulder-length. She actively gestures toward the graph, pointing to the 2020 spike and the subsequent decline, emphasizing the data with dynamic hand movements and expressive body language. Her voice explains that while wages have recently increased and inflation is tapering, there are still "three years of inflation scars" in the economy. She then discusses economic sentiment, citing a University of Michigan poll showing people 55 and older feeling better due to exposure to record high stock and home prices, contrasting this with younger people who still face economic troubles, linking it to the Biden administration's aggressive student loan forgiveness efforts. The studio environment remains consistent with blue-lit panels and a professional news set aesthetic, and the lower third text remains unchanged, reinforcing the headline about the March unemployment rate.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with Christine Romans, NBC News Senior Business Correspondent, standing in the Studio Environment beside a large screen displaying the 'Unemployment Rate' graph, showing a decline to 3.8% in March, with the 'INFLATION IN AMERICA' tag. A Male News Anchor's voice is heard saying, "Very good point, absolutely. Christine, thank you very much. Appreciate the expertise." The scene transitions to a wide shot of the studio as the Male News Anchor continues, "The clock is ticking to get your eclipse glasses and make your Monday afternoon plans because, as you probably already know, have you heard about it? A total solar eclipse is coming to America." The Large Screen Display cycles through graphics, including a black-and-white image of a collapsed bridge, a list of impacts, and the 'Hallie Jackson NOW' logo, before showing a 'U.S. Stock Market at Close' graphic with positive gains. The Male News Anchor describes the eclipse as a "once in a generation, maybe once in a lifetime event" where the moon fully blocks the sun's light, creating darkness for a few minutes. The screen then shifts to a blue 'CLOCK TICKING' graphic with eclipse glasses, followed by a dramatic solar eclipse visual with 'TOTAL ECLIPSE' and 'TOURISTS FLOCK TO LOCATIONS ALONG THE SOLAR ECLIPSE PATH OF TOTALITY' on the lower third. The Male News Anchor explains that the 'ECLIPSE PATH OF TOTALITY' runs through more than a dozen states, turning them into "huge tourist attractions." The Large Screen Display shows a map of the 'ECLIPSE PATH OF TOTALITY' across the U.S., highlighting cities like Buffalo, Cleveland, Indianapolis, and Dallas. The segment transitions to a split-screen interview in the Studio Environment, with the Male News Anchor on the left and Priscilla Thompson, NBC News Correspondent, on the right, standing in a restaurant in Grapevine, TX. The Male News Anchor introduces her, "Let's bring in NBC's Priscilla Thompson, who's in one of those areas, just out of Dallas. Priscilla, Dallas is one of the first cities that will be in the zone, as it were, when this eclipse starts in Mexico, right? And I hear tourists are flooding in already." Priscilla, wearing a yellow sleeveless top, gestures as she responds, "Yeah, Tom, there are 83 counties here in Texas that are going to be in that path of totality, and tourists are already starting to make their way in." The lower third graphic consistently displays 'TOURISTS FLOCK TO LOCATIONS ALONG THE SOLAR ECLIPSE PATH OF TOTALITY', and the NBC News Now logo is visible throughout.

The scene transitions to a wide shot of the studio, where the Large Screen Display cycles through various graphics, including a black-and-white image of a collapsed bridge with 'PROMISING MORE AID' overlaid, followed by a list of impacts from the bridge collapse, and then the 'Hallie Jackson NOW' logo. The screen then shows a full-screen graphic titled 'U.S. Stock Market at Close' with green upward arrows and positive gains for the DOW, S&P 500, and NASDAQ. The segment shifts to a blue screen with the text 'CLOCK TICKING' and a black-and-white image of hands holding eclipse glasses, followed by a dramatic visual of a solar eclipse with the text 'TOTAL ECLIPSE' and 'TOURISTS FLOCK TO LOCATIONS ALONG THE SOLAR ECLIPSE PATH OF TOTALITY'. The Large Screen Display then shows a map of the 'ECLIPSE PATH OF TOTALITY' across the United States, highlighting cities like Buffalo, NY, Cleveland, OH, Indianapolis, IN, and Dallas, TX. The scene transitions to a split-screen interview in the Studio Environment, with the Male News Anchor on the left and Priscilla Thompson, NBC News Correspondent, on the right, standing in a restaurant in Grapevine, TX, as indicated by the on-screen text. Priscilla, wearing a yellow sleeveless top, gestures as she speaks, while the Male News Anchor, in a dark suit and light blue checkered shirt, listens and responds. The lower third graphic consistently displays 'TOURISTS FLOCK TO LOCATIONS ALONG THE SOLAR ECLIPSE PATH OF TOTALITY', and the NBC News Now logo is visible in the lower right corner throughout.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with NBC News Correspondent Priscilla Thompson, wearing a bright yellow sleeveless top, reporting animatedly from a well-lit restaurant in Grapevine, TX, with on-screen text confirming the location and time as 4:22 PM, and highlighting 'TOURISTS FLOCK TO LOCATIONS ALONG THE SOLAR ECLIPSE PATH OF TOTALITY' alongside the 'TOTAL ECLIPSE' graphic and NBC News Now logo. Priscilla's voice explains the surge in tourism, noting that hotels and rentals along the path of totality, including the Hotel Vin in Grapevine, are almost entirely booked, offering specialty foods and drinks. The scene transitions to a map graphic titled 'APRIL 8TH ECLIPSE BOOKINGS', illustrating U.S. occupancy rates with a dense orange band marking the path of totality, sourced from AIRDNA. Returning to Priscilla at the bar, she interacts with two bartenders, Patty (a woman in a black shirt with long hair) and Daniel (a man in a black 'COURY' shirt), who prepare colorful drinks. Patty's voice describes a "day and night drink" (a Chilton with a black salt rim), a "rum mule" called "Dark and Stormy", and a "Ring of Fire" with jalapeno vodka. Daniel's voice then details their "Totality My Tai," a traditional Mai Tai with white rum, Orgeat, orange curacao, lime juice, and a dark rum float. Priscilla thanks them, noting this is a taste of what tourists will experience, and transitions to a clip from the hotel manager. The segment concludes with Kalyana Krishnamoorthy, Hotel Manager of Hotel Vin, appearing in a professional setting in a gray blazer, speaking directly to the camera about the "international" aspect of the influx of visitors, with his lower third and the 'TOTAL ECLIPSE' graphic visible.
segments.visual_original_caption: The segment opens with Priscilla Thompson, an NBC News Correspondent, standing in a well-lit restaurant in Grapevine, TX, wearing a bright yellow sleeveless top and gesturing animatedly as she reports on the surge of tourists flocking to locations along the solar eclipse path of totality. The on-screen text reads 'GRAPEVINE, TX 4:22 PM' and 'TOURISTS FLOCK TO LOCATIONS ALONG THE SOLAR ECLIPSE PATH OF TOTALITY' with the 'TOTAL ECLIPSE' graphic and NBC News Now logo visible. The scene transitions to a map graphic titled 'APRIL 8TH ECLIPSE BOOKINGS' showing U.S. occupancy rates from 10% to 100%, with a dense orange band highlighting the path of totality, sourced from AIRDNA. The video then cuts back to Priscilla at the bar, where she interacts with two bartenders — a woman in a black shirt with long hair and a man in a black 'COURY' shirt — as they prepare colorful drinks, including a layered cocktail and a copper mug drink, while she continues her report. The setting is warm and inviting, with shelves of liquor bottles and ambient lighting. The segment concludes with a cut to Kalyana Krishnamoorthy, Hotel Manager of Hotel Vin, who appears in a professional setting, wearing a gray blazer over a checkered shirt, speaking directly to the camera about the eclipse-related tourism surge. The lower third identifies him clearly, and the 'TOTAL ECLIPSE' graphic remains on screen throughout.

segments.integrated_caption: The video segment begins with Kalyana Krishnamoorthy, Hotel Manager of Hotel Vin, speaking directly to the camera in a professional indoor setting. Dressed in a gray blazer over a checkered shirt, he explains the high demand for reservations from both international and domestic visitors, noting that calls are still coming in for bookings and emphasizing the hotel's efforts to "curate an experience" for guests arriving for the entire weekend leading up to Monday's eclipse. A distinct chime sound accompanies a transition to a blue screen displaying 'NBC NEWS HALLIE JACKSON'. The scene then shifts to NBC News Correspondent Priscilla Thompson, wearing a bright yellow sleeveless top, reporting live from a bustling restaurant in Grapevine, TX, at 4:23 PM. She gestures expressively as she details how the hotel has spent three years developing special bourbon bottles as souvenirs, underscoring the extensive preparation by local businesses for this "incredible moment and this incredible influx of visitors." The segment transitions to a split-screen interview with the Male News Anchor in the Studio Environment, who, dressed in a dark suit, playfully suggests "off camera" that one should "test out these tourist attractions." Priscilla, now holding a layered cocktail and smiling while interacting with bartenders behind the bar, agrees, joking that she'll "get a little sip" after her last live shot. The Male News Anchor then asks if some people are "superstitious about a total eclipse," to which Priscilla briefly responds, "Yeah." Throughout the report, the lower third consistently displays the 'TOTAL ECLIPSE' graphic and the headline about tourists flocking to eclipse locations, with the lively restaurant ambiance audible in the background during Priscilla's reports.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a dynamic blue and black graphic introducing 'The Five Things' segment on NBC News Now, featuring a collage of images including a hot air balloon crash scene, a basketball player, a P&G logo, and an airport security line. A male voice states, "All right, let's get you over now to The Five Things our team thinks you should know about tonight." The segment transitions to a detailed report on a fatal hot air balloon crash, showing aerial footage of a collapsed balloon with red, yellow, and gray fabric spread across a dry, sandy field. Several individuals are seen inspecting the wreckage near a white pickup truck and a trailer carrying the wicker basket. A City of El Paso police truck is shown parked near the crash site, with yellow caution tape cordoning off the area. The lower third graphic '#1 ERROR FOUND IN AUTOPSY REPORT OF PILOT INVOLVED IN FATAL HOT AIR BALLOON CRASH' appears as the male voice reports, "Number one, a medical examiner is correcting the initial autopsy report for the pilot in that fatal Arizona hot air balloon crash. The original report said the pilot had ketamine in his system, and the drug was not used in resuscitation efforts after the crash, but now county authorities say medics did in fact give him ketamine while transporting him to the hospital for resuscitation efforts. You'll recall four people died after the balloon fell roughly 2,000 feet back in January." The scene then shifts to 'FILE' footage of an airport security checkpoint, where travelers move through TSA screening with conveyor belts and metal detectors. Overhead signs warn against bringing firearms, and TSA agents in blue uniforms monitor the process. The lower third graphic '#2 WASHINGTON POST: HUNDREDS OF PEOPLE BYPASSED PARTS OF AIRPORT SECURITY WITHIN LAST YEAR' is displayed as the male voice continues, "Number two, the TSA tells the Washington Post that hundreds of people have bypassed airport security measures in the past 12 months, doing things like sneaking through ID checkpoints and going the wrong direction through one-way exit lanes. A TSA spokesperson says most of these were really bypasses that are rather rare, are inadvertent and unintentional actions."

segments.integrated_caption: The segment begins with close-ups of the blue 'P&G' logo on a white building facade, transitioning to the full 'Procter & Gamble' logo on a studio screen with a blue digital background. A male voice announces, 'Number three, Procter & Gamble recalling more than 8 million defective bags of laundry detergent pods,' explaining a 'packaging flaw that could hurt children' and listing 'Tide Pods, Gain Flings, Ace Pods, and Ariel Pods' made between last September and this February. The scene then shifts to the exterior of a 99 Cent Only Stores location with a purple sign and brown facade, followed by interior footage of a checkout line where a cashier bags groceries. The male voice continues, 'Number four, 99 Cents Only Stores are shutting down all 371 locations,' correcting himself from 'CSO' to 'CEO' and attributing the closure to 'factors like the pandemic and shifting consumer demand,' noting it was 'very, extremely difficult' to make the decision, with the chain liquidating 'all of its merchandise.' The lower third updates to 'The Five Things #4: 99 CENT ONLY STORES SHUTTER ALL LOCATIONS'. Finally, the segment cuts to a basketball player in a red USC jersey with number 2, then Bronny James in a white USC jersey with number 6 walking on an indoor court, with his father LeBron James visible in the stands. The male voice concludes, 'Number five, USC freshman Bronny James, LeBron James's son, will enter the 2024 NBA Draft, as well as the college transfer portal.' A lower third graphic displays '#5 BRONNY JAMES TO ENTER NBA DRAFT, TRANSFER PORTAL'. The scene transitions to a close-up of Bronny James in a white USC practice shirt, standing on the court with teammates in red jackets behind him, as a male news anchor's voice begins, explaining, "Now remember, over the summer, Bronny suffered cardiac arrest while at a basketball workout and had to have a heart procedure that sidelined him for several months." The scene then cuts to a 'COMING UP' preview, first showing a woman in a gray t-shirt with the number '163' pinned to it, labeled 'MOTHER'S FIGHT', followed by a mugshot of a bald man in an orange prison jumpsuit with a goatee, labeled 'STOLEN IDENTITY'. The anchor continues, introducing these stories: "In just a bit from us, our NBC News digital documentary showing a mother's fight to save her son, held hostage by Hamas. And a decades-long case of stolen identity is giving shades of Don Draper. We're coming back." The broadcast shifts to the male news anchor, seated in a modern studio with blue-lit ceilings and marble flooring, speaking directly to the camera with a screen behind him displaying 'Hallie Jackson NOW'. He states, "Bottom of the hour, and NBC News covers hundreds of stories every day. It is awfully tough to read, watch, or listen to everything." The scene cuts to a wide studio shot featuring a large screen displaying 'the local' with a collage of images including the mugshot, two women, a solar eclipse, and a park scene, as the anchor gestures toward it, continuing, "So our bureau teams have identified some highlights. Here's what they say is going on down in their regions. We call the segment 'the local'." The focus then narrows to a full-screen view of the mugshot, with the lower third text 'WISCONSIN MAN PLEADS GUILTY TO STEALING ANOTHER MAN'S IDENTITY FOR THREE DECADES'. The anchor elaborates, "All right, from our Midwest bureau, a Wisconsin man has pleaded guilty to using a fake identity, listen to this, for more than 30 years. According to a plea deal, the man used the victim's identity in every aspect of his..." The NBC News Now logo remains visible in the lower right corner throughout.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a mugshot of a bald man in an orange prison jumpsuit with a goatee, identified as a Wisconsin man who pleaded guilty to stealing another man's identity for three decades, with the narrator detailing how the actual victim was falsely imprisoned for identity theft and ended up in a psychiatric hospital before police used DNA to uncover the truth. The scene then shifts to a split-screen mugshot of a blonde woman and a younger woman with long brown hair, as the narrator reports their arrest in Texas for performing illegal butt injections across the US without a medical license, revealing they attempted the procedure on an undercover officer. This is visually emphasized by a close-up of several tubes of silicone sealant and caulk, including brands like DAP and Silicone Max, displayed on a dark surface. The focus then moves to an exterior shot of the red-brick Hudson Correctional Facility in New York, followed by dramatic solar eclipse footage, including a partial eclipse behind the Statue of Liberty and a full annular eclipse. The narrator explains that six inmates at this New York State prison won the right to watch Monday's solar eclipse after suing over the state's decision to lock down prisons for safety, arguing it violated their constitutional rights. The segment concludes with a blue screen featuring the NBC News Now logo as the narration fades out, mentioning the prison is looking into other religious requests.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens in a modern news studio with blue-lit ceilings and marble flooring, featuring a large screen displaying a vertical text animation of 'NBC NEWS NOW_' and a list of topics including 'MARKETS' and 'ECLIPSE'. A male anchor's voice introduces new reporting from NBC News Digital Docs, focusing on a mother's fight to bring her son home after he was taken hostage by Hamas on October 7th. The screen transitions to a black-and-white image under the headline 'MOTHER'S FIGHT', showing a woman and a man at a press conference with microphones and photos of a young man displayed. The anchor identifies the hostage as "23-year-old Hersh Goldberg-Polin" and the mother as "Rachel Goldberg-Polin," who has become a vocal advocate. The scene shifts to personal photos of a smiling young man with a beard, first on a boat and then with a woman in a park, both accompanied by the lower third 'MOTHER OF HOSTAGE TAKEN BY HAMAS FIGHTS FOR HER SON'S RETURN HOME'. The focus then moves to Rachel Goldberg-Polin, speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit room with large windows, wearing a gray t-shirt with the number '163' pinned to it. Her voice, slightly strained and emotional, states, "Today is day 27. Hersh is still not home. 52, 75, 86. Today is day 100 since my son was stolen from me." A melancholic instrumental track plays softly in the background as she is shown writing the number '100' on a piece of tape, with the text 'TODAY IS DAY 100 SINCE' appearing on screen, emphasizing the passage of time through close-ups of her hands. She continues, "It's sad to be known for something really painful and really scary. And that's what people think of when they see me." The segment concludes with her seated at a desk with dual monitors, continuing her work, while the lower third remains consistent with the headline about her son’s captivity.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of hands, identified as Rachel Goldberg-Polin's, writing the number '163' on masking tape, which she then affixes to her gray t-shirt. Her voice explains, "They would say, 'How many days has it been?' So I just took masking tape and started to make these numbers," adding that it makes people question, "How are we allowing this to continue to happen?" The scene transitions to Rachel seated at a desk with dual monitors, scissors, tape, and papers, before a black screen displays the title 'STOLEN SON: A HOSTAGE'S MOM FIGHTS TO BRING HER CHILD HOME FROM GAZA' from NBC News Digital Docs, accompanied by somber, reflective piano music. Rachel is then seen walking hand-in-hand with a man through a cobblestone European square, possibly Vatican City, before she speaks directly to the camera in a dimly lit room, her expression somber and resolute, still wearing the '163' pin. Over the continuing music, she states, "The hostage families have to pretend to be normal. If we want to function, and if we want to save them." Archival photos of her son, Hersh, appear—first smiling at a music festival, then in a black-and-white image with text explaining he was injured and taken hostage during the October 7, 2023, Hamas attack, specifically noting his left arm was blown off below the elbow. Rachel is shown wiping her eyes, visibly emotional, before regaining her composure as she concludes, "First thing in the morning, I always say to myself, 'And now pretend to be human.' And I get up." Throughout the segment, a lower third graphic consistently displays 'MOTHER OF HOSTAGE TAKEN BY HAMAS FIGHTS FOR HER SON'S RETURN HOME' with the 'ISRAEL-HAMAS WAR' tag and NBC News Now logo.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with Rachel Goldberg-Polin, a woman with dark hair pulled back, wearing a gray t-shirt with the number '163' pinned to it, speaking emotionally in a dimly lit room with large windows and modern furnishings. Her voice, filled with a mix of distress and determination, describes her daily struggle, stating, "I put on this costume of being a person." She recounts a specific walk she does in the mornings, crafted "so that I don't see anybody," walking with her head down, wearing sunglasses, and often a hood, even crossing the street to avoid people. She continues, expressing the constant emotional burden, "all day long, it's kind of like someone's holding a branding iron on your back and you can't let anybody know." The lower third graphic reads 'MOTHER OF HOSTAGE TAKEN BY HAMAS FIGHTS FOR HER SON'S RETURN HOME' with the 'ISRAEL-HAMAS WAR' tag, while 'NBC NEWS DIGITAL DOCS' and 'NBC NEWS NOW' logos are visible. The scene transitions to her walking hand-in-hand with a man through a European-style square, then reading from a book in a quiet, reflective moment, all while her poignant voiceover continues. A camera shutter sound briefly punctuates the audio before she states, "I was asked early on if I would speak at the United Nations in New York, and so I did." The video then cuts to a press conference at the United Nations Security Council on 'Oct. 24, 2023' in 'New York City', where she stands at a podium with a UN microphone, flanked by several individuals, including a man in a dark suit shaking hands with another man. Her voice becomes more urgent as she passionately asks, "Why is no one crying out for these people to be allowed access to the Red Cross? Why is no one demanding just proof of life?" She concludes this thought by emphasizing, "We felt that the advocacy for the hostage situation was so important." The group behind her listens intently, and the scene shifts to her speaking at another podium with a UN backdrop, this time in a more formal setting with a large banner reading 'HUMAN RIGHTS 10-11-12 DECEMBER 2024', and finally speaking into a microphone at a different event with a large UN emblem, reinforcing her ongoing advocacy.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a wide shot of a protest in front of the U.S. Capitol, where demonstrators hold signs and American and Israeli flags, underscoring a political rally related to the Israel-Hamas war, with chants of "Bring them home!" audible. The scene transitions to a close-up of Rachel Goldberg-Polin, a woman with dark hair pulled back, wearing sunglasses and a yellow top, speaking passionately at a podium flanked by U.S. and Israeli flags. The setting shifts to an indoor environment with large windows, where she is seen working intently on a laptop, wearing a gray t-shirt with the number '163' pinned to it, symbolizing her son’s hostage number. She then speaks directly to the camera in a dimly lit room, her expression earnest and emotional, recounting her fight for her son’s return, stating, "I've had episodes where I do break. It's better when I know that it's coming and I can, you know, say, 'I'll be right back' and, you know, go to my room or go to the bathroom or go somewhere where it's not so public. But that's not the way trauma works. So there are definitely times where I'm out there screaming or crying or lying on the street." The video cuts to harrowing footage from Deir al-Balah, Gaza, showing chaotic scenes of civilians, including a woman carrying a baby, fleeing amidst destruction, with an ambulance visible in the background and a siren wailing. The final frames depict a distressed infant being attended to by medical personnel on the ground, highlighting the human toll of the conflict, as Rachel adds, "I'm also extremely heartbroken over the unbelievable suffering of hundreds of thousands of innocent Gazans." Throughout, the lower third graphic reads 'MOTHER OF HOSTAGE TAKEN BY HAMAS FIGHTS FOR HER SON'S RETURN HOME,' with the 'ISRAEL-HAMAS WAR' tag and NBC News Now branding consistently displayed.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with chaotic, handheld footage of civilians fleeing through rubble in Gaza, carrying belongings and children, amid destroyed buildings and dust, highlighting the devastation of the Israel-Hamas war. Over these scenes, Rachel Goldberg-Polin's emotional voice describes the "unbearable" and "so painful to watch" suffering of "hundreds of thousands of people" on "both sides," asserting "it's time for the suffering to stop." The scene shifts to a somber memorial in a barren field where a woman kneels before a photo of a missing person, surrounded by Israeli flags and other portraits, accompanied by the distinct sounds of a shofar and chanting. This transitions to a protest where Rachel Goldberg-Polin, wearing a gray t-shirt with '163' pinned to it, holds a sign with her son's photo and the plea 'BRING HIM HOME NOW!', surrounded by others holding similar signs. Her voice continues, intimately recounting how she sits "cross-legged right in front of this big poster" of her son, looking into "his eyes" and telling "him" to "stay strong mentally, spiritually, psychologically, physically, religiously," to "survive," and reassuring him, "You have no choice. We are coming. You can make it through this and you're going to have a long beautiful life." Interspersed with her powerful words are personal, joyful archival footage of the young man—smiling, dancing, and celebrating—credited to the 'Goldberg-Polin Family', depicting him shirtless on a rocky cliff, in a colorful shirt on a boat, and dancing in a living room with disco lights. The segment concludes with a family photo of the young man with his parents on a boat, followed by a transition to the NBC News studio, where the Male News Anchor, in a dark suit, introduces the story with a serious demeanor, seated in front of a screen displaying 'Hallie Jackson NOW'. The lower third consistently reads 'MOTHER OF HOSTAGE TAKEN BY HAMAS FIGHTS FOR HER SON'S RETURN HOME', with the 'ISRAEL-HAMAS WAR' tag and NBC News Now logo visible, alongside the 'NBC News Digital Docs' branding and the title 'STOLEN SON: A HOSTAGE'S MOM FIGHTS TO BRING HER CHILD HOME FROM GAZA'.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a dynamic studio shot of the NBC News Now set, featuring a large screen displaying a blue graphic with the text 'THE FIVE THINGS' and a list of topics including 'HOSTAGE', 'DIDDY', 'BASKETBALL', 'NEW YORK CITY', and 'TAIWAN'. A male anchor then introduces an "NBC News exclusive" as the camera focuses on a large screen showing a black-and-white image of Diddy and his son Christian Combs at the VMAs, with the text 'SON NOW ACCUSED'. The anchor states that "the son of recording artist Sean Diddy Combs" is "also now accused of sexual assault." The scene transitions to a live performance clip of Christian Combs, identified as "26-year-old Christian Combs," on stage, wearing a white t-shirt and a thick chain, smiling and gesturing with his hands. A lower third graphic appears, stating 'NBC NEWS EXCLUSIVE: NEW LAWSUIT ACCUSES DIDDY'S SON CHRISTIAN OF SEXUALLY ASSAULTING WOMAN ON A YACHT'. The anchor elaborates that the lawsuit, "first obtained by NBC News," accuses Christian Combs of sexually assaulting a woman in December 2022, who "thought she was being hired for a wholesome family excursion on a yacht that Diddy charted," but "instead, she found a quote 'hedonistic environment'." The video then cuts to a close-up of legal documents, with a quote overlay: 'DEFENDANT S. COMBS turned what was sold as a wholesome family excursion into a hedonistic environment.' The anchor adds that "Diddy is named in the lawsuit as well, accused of aiding and abetting his son." The scene shifts back to the VMAs image, followed by a graphic with a statement from Aaron Dyer, lawyer for Sean and Christian Combs, dismissing the lawsuit as 'lewd and meritless' and stating they will file a motion to dismiss. The anchor confirms that "in the last couple of hours, we've heard from a lawyer for Diddy and his son, calling this a lewd and meritless claim from the accuser's lawyer, saying father and son will file a motion to dismiss the lawsuit." The segment concludes with a split-screen showing aerial footage of Diddy’s Los Angeles mansion and a van in Miami, both dated March 25, alongside a performance clip of Diddy on stage, as the anchor references "dramatic police raids earlier this month at Diddy's home in Los Angeles and also Miami," and notes that "Diddy has denied all allegations."

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a performance clip of Diddy, wearing a black jacket, sunglasses, and a diamond cross necklace, holding a microphone against a red-lit background, while a lower third banner announces 'NBC NEWS EXCLUSIVE: NEW LAWSUIT ACCUSES DIDDY'S SON CHRISTIAN OF SEXUALLY ASSAULTING WOMAN ON A YACHT'. This transitions to a full-screen blue graphic displaying 'Hallie Jackson NOW_'. The broadcast then cuts to a split-screen studio interview where a Male News Anchor, in a dark suit and light blue checkered shirt, speaks from the Studio Environment, with a screen behind him showing 'Hallie Jackson NOW'. He discusses the 'wave of sexual assault and sex trafficking allegations that he faces' (referring to Diddy) and introduces Chloe Melas, asking, 'Chloe, what else can you tell us about this lawsuit against Christian and Diddy Combs and did this come as a surprise?' On the right, Chloe Melas, the NBC News Entertainment Correspondent, appears in a light pink blazer over a ruffled blouse, seated in front of a blue screen also displaying 'Hallie Jackson NOW_'. Her lower third identifies her as 'CHLOE MELAS, NBC NEWS ENTERTAINMENT CORRESPONDENT' and reiterates the lawsuit headline. Chloe responds, 'Nice to see you,' and proceeds to detail the allegations: 'this woman is accusing Christian Combs of an incident that allegedly took place in December 2022 just days before Diddy had a really big party on this yacht for New Year's Eve.' She explains the victim claims she was 'drugged and sexually assaulted by Christian Combs,' noting that Christian Combs's lawyer is 'denying these allegations.' Chloe adds that the victim claims there are 'audio recordings that were taken by a music producer... who was always recording things that were going on and just happened to catch some of the exchanges between Christian Combs and this alleged victim that they believe backs up her claims,' though she clarifies 'NBC News has not been able to independently verify that.' During Chloe's explanation, the visual shifts to a full-screen graphic titled 'NBC News Exclusive: Son of Sean 'Diddy' Combs Accused of Sexual Assault_', detailing the allegations against Christian Combs (drugging and sexually assaulting a victim on a yacht in December 2022, arriving heavily intoxicated, giving inappropriate attention, and groping the victim) and Sean 'Diddy' Combs (sued for premises liability and allegedly aiding and abetting the assault), with photos of both men flanking the blue panel and the NBC News logo at the bottom left.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a static graphic titled 'Timeline of Lawsuits Against Sean 'Diddy' Combs_', detailing five legal actions from November 2023 to April 2024, including Cassie's sexual abuse lawsuit, a Jane Doe rape allegation, a former producer's 'RICO enterprise' accusation, the FBI search of Diddy's homes, and a lawsuit accusing his son of assault. A photo of Sean 'Diddy' Combs in a white jacket and sunglasses is on the right, with a dark background and orange glow, and the NBC News Now logo in the lower right. The scene then transitions to a live studio where Hallie Jackson, in a light pink blazer and pearl necklace, speaks directly to the camera. The lower third reads 'NBC NEWS EXCLUSIVE' and 'NEW LAWSUIT ACCUSES DIDDY OF AIDING AND ABETTING HIS SON IN SEX ASSAULT ON A YACHT'. Hallie Jackson elaborates on the timeline, explaining that Cassie and two others sued Diddy for sexual abuse in November 2023, which Diddy denied but later settled. She continues, detailing the December 2023 allegation by a Jane Doe who claimed she was raped by Diddy at 17, and the February 2024 lawsuit from a former record producer accusing Diddy of various offenses including assault. Hallie then references the March 2024 FBI raids on Diddy's homes, noting that he has not been criminally charged and the findings are unknown. She concludes by mentioning the April 2024 lawsuit, which NBC News exclusively obtained. The scene cuts to a split-screen, with Hallie Jackson on the right and a male news anchor in a dark suit on the left, who listens intently and occasionally nods. Both are in a modern studio with blue-lit ceilings and 'Hallie Jackson NOW_' branding on screens, while the lower third remains unchanged. Hallie concludes by stating that 'things are not looking good' and it will be interesting to see what happens in the coming weeks.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a split-screen interview in a modern news studio, featuring the Male News Anchor in a dark suit and light blue checkered shirt alongside Hallie Jackson, who wears a light pink blazer and pearl necklace, as the Male News Anchor thanks a previous guest and introduces the upcoming segment. The studio, characterized by blue-lit ceilings and marble flooring, displays 'Hallie Jackson NOW_' on multiple screens. A 'COMING UP' preview then appears, showcasing a golden NCAA logo, 'MARCH MADNESS' text, and a smartphone with the NCAA app, as the Male News Anchor highlights the soaring ticket prices for the Women's Final Four and quotes the NCAA's president on the sport's success. This transitions to a full-screen blue graphic with 'Hallie Jackson NOW_' before returning to the studio view, where a large screen displays 'THE FIVE THINGS' and a list of topics including 'HOSTAGE', 'DIDDY', 'BASKETBALL', 'NEW YORK CITY', 'TAIWAN', 'BIDEN', and 'ISRAEL'. The focus shifts to a large screen showing 'WOMEN'S FINAL FOUR' with a black-and-white NCAA logo, followed by a detailed bracket for the Women's March Madness Final Four, listing 1 South Carolina, 3 NC State, 1 Iowa, and 3 UConn, with game times at 7pm and 9:30pm. The Male News Anchor excitedly announces they are hours away from the most anticipated Women's Final Four ever, detailing the matchups: South Carolina against North Carolina State, and a 'heavyweight bout' featuring UConn's Paige Bueckers and Iowa's Caitlin Clark. Dynamic basketball action footage then plays, showing a UConn player in a black jersey with number 5 (Paige Bueckers) and an Iowa player in a white jersey with number 22 (Caitlin Clark). The segment continues with a graphic titled 'Iowa, LSU Shatters Women's Basketball Viewership Record', showing 12.3 million viewers for the 2024 Elite Eight Game, surpassing the previous record of 9.9 million, as the Male News Anchor notes that experts expect more records after 12 million tuned in for Monday's LSU-Iowa game. Finally, a graphic titled 'Women's NCAA Tournament Final Could Break Another Record' presents expert viewership predictions: 16.9 million for Iowa vs. South Carolina and 14.1 million for Iowa vs. UConn, with 10 million for NC State vs. South Carolina and 8 million for NC State vs. UConn. The NBC News Now logo remains visible in the lower right corner throughout.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with NBC News Correspondent Jesse Kirsch, wearing a navy blue jacket, reporting live from Cleveland in front of a glass building adorned with '2024 Women's Final Four Cleveland' banners. He speaks animatedly, discussing the high energy around the games and the rising ticket prices, noting that according to TickPick, the average purchase price is $572 (96% more than last year) and the 'Get-in' price is $451 (401% more). The lower third displays 'MARCH MADNESS_' and 'TONIGHT: WOMEN'S FINAL FOUR TIPS OFF AS EXPERTS PREDICT NEW TV RECORDS', transitioning to a graphic titled 'Most Expensive Women's Final Four Ever_' with this data. Jesse then introduces NCAA officials, including the Vice President for Women's Basketball. The scene shifts to an indoor basketball arena where Jesse interviews Lynn Holzman, NCAA VP of Women's Basketball, who, in a navy blazer, gestures as she explains that the sport's rise is a 'combination of both exposure through TV, but it's social media. Our players out there, their personalities are coming out. We have great coaches that are faces of the game as well. The skill and athleticism on the court is phenomenal. These are great players, the basketball skill, the power that they have.' The lower third identifies her and states 'NCAA WOMEN'S BASKETBALL VP: COMBINATION OF TV EXPOSURE, SOCIAL MEDIA HAS FUELED RISE'. The camera returns to Jesse Kirsch outside, who continues, 'We're looking at a lot of star power across this game, but of course, one name continues to stick out, Tom, and that's Caitlin Clark.' The final shot is a split-screen, with Jesse on the right and a Male News Anchor in a dark suit on the left in a studio environment, holding a phone and responding, 'Yeah, Jesse, I've got all for...' The NBC News Now logo is visible throughout.
