segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a split-screen view in a modern news studio, where a Male News Anchor, seated in a dark suit, addresses Jesse Kirsch, an NBC News Correspondent standing outdoors in Cleveland. The anchor highlights the disparity in venue sizes, noting women play in 18,000-seat arenas while men's Final Four uses 70,000-seat football stadiums, questioning when parity might be achieved. Jesse, wearing a navy blue jacket, stands before a glass building adorned with '2024 Women's Final Four Cleveland' banners, with the lower third displaying 'MARCH MADNESS_' and 'NCAA WOMEN'S BASKETBALL VP: COMBINATION OF TV EXPOSURE, SOCIAL MEDIA HAS FUELED RISE'. The scene transitions to a full-screen view of Jesse Kirsch, who, with animated gestures, responds to the anchor's point, recalling past disparities like weight room facilities at the 2021 tournament. The lower third updates to 'TONIGHT: WOMEN'S FINAL FOUR TIPS OFF AS EXPERTS PREDICT NEW TV RECORDS' as pedestrians and vehicles move in the background. Jesse explains that the NCAA President and Vice President have discussed investments to bring parity to both men's and women's tournaments, including planning for larger venues for women's events years in advance, as the expectation is for the game to continue growing. The segment then cuts to an indoor basketball court, marked with 'NCAA WOMEN'S' branding, where Jesse interviews Lynn Holzman, NCAA VP of Women's Basketball, in a navy blazer, and NCAA President Charlie Baker, in a dark suit. Jesse introduces a clip from the president, who is then heard beginning to speak, stating, 'I want to think about this as...'

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a split-screen interview on a basketball court during the 2024 NCAA Women's Tournament, featuring Jesse Kirsch, Lynn Holzman, and Charlie Baker. NCAA President Charlie Baker, in a dark suit and light blue tie, gestures expressively as he states, "I see women's basketball's future as something where there is no peak," emphasizing that it's "just going to keep going up" and highlighting the goal to make investments and changes despite long-term planning. The setting shows 'NCAA WOMEN'S' branding on the court and empty red seats. The scene transitions to Jesse Kirsch, an NBC News Correspondent, reporting live from Cleveland, standing in front of a glass building adorned with '2024 Women's Final Four Cleveland' banners. He gestures while speaking, addressing "Tom" and highlighting that over "12 million people... tuned in on Monday night for a quarter final," making it the "most watched women's college basketball game ever." Background music then begins as the video cuts to a blue graphic with scrolling categories like 'TRAVEL', 'SPORTS', 'WORLD', and 'CLIMATE', followed by a black-and-white shot of a woman holding an umbrella and looking at her phone, with 'SEE ALL' and 'WASHINGTON DC' text. Next, a male news anchor appears in a modern studio with blue-lit ceiling and marble flooring, standing beside a large screen displaying headlines including 'BRIDGE COLLAPSE', 'EARTHQUAKE', 'ECONOMY', and 'GAZA'. The anchor, in a dark suit and light blue checkered shirt, delivers breaking news, stating they are "just now learning about a body being found unidentified at the Baltimore Bridge collapse." He then reports on "New York City, all shook up" after a "really surprising and rare earthquake," as the screen shows surveillance footage labeled 'CITY SHOOK UP' from a store in New Jersey, capturing shelves shaking and patrons reacting to the earthquake.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with News Anchor Tom Costello, dressed in a dark suit and light blue checkered shirt, standing in a modern studio environment with blue-lit ceilings and marble flooring. He gestures toward a large screen displaying surveillance footage labeled 'CITY SHOOK UP' from a New Jersey store, showing shelves and tables shaking, as he discusses "tremors all across the Northeast, the strongest quake New Jersey has felt in 250 years." The screen then transitions to a blue background listing headlines like 'BRIDGE COLLAPSE', 'EARTHQUAKE', 'ECONOMY', and 'GAZA', while Costello talks about a "new jobs report [that] came in very hot, historic gains." He then introduces a 'HOT REPORT' segment featuring a 'HELP WANTED' sign for 'Rose Paving Co.' seeking an Asphalt Luteman and Pavebox Operator. The screen shifts to a black-and-white image of a woman under the headline 'FIGHT TO GET HOME', followed by a black-and-white image of Diddy with the text 'NEW CHARGES', as Costello announces an "NBC News exclusive on the mounting charges coming now for Sean Diddy Combs and another member of his family, his son." The screen cycles through various headlines, including 'ECLIPSE COUNTDOWN', showing children wearing eclipse glasses and a stack of viewing cards, with Costello noting, "the countdown is on... for this once in a generation event." The segment concludes with Costello speaking directly to the camera, identifying himself as Tom Costello and stating, "I am in for Hallie," with the studio background visible, including a screen displaying 'Hallie Jackson NOW' and the NBC News Now logo in the lower right corner.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens in a modern news studio with Male News Anchor Tom, dressed in a dark suit, standing beside a large screen displaying the 'Hallie Jackson NOW_' graphic, holding papers and gesturing as a 'JUST IN: BODY FOUND AT THE SITE OF BALTIMORE BRIDGE COLLAPSE' banner scrolls below. Tom reports that a body has been found at the tragic Baltimore Bridge collapse site, according to the family of the deceased. The screen transitions to aerial footage of the collapsed bridge, showing a large container ship with 'MAERSK' containers colliding with the steel truss structure, causing debris in the water. Tom continues, narrating President Biden's key trip to Baltimore to tour the wreckage and his private meeting with the families of the six men killed when the massive cargo ship plowed into the Francis Scott Key Bridge 10 days ago. The scene cuts to President Biden, wearing a blue puffer jacket and black cap, speaking at a podium with the Presidential Seal in Baltimore, flanked by officials. Biden's voice is heard declaring, "I'm here to say, your nation has your back and I mean it. Your nation has your back." The video returns to the studio, where Tom discusses ongoing salvage and recovery efforts, and the large screen displays a photo of Petty Officer 2nd Class Taylor Bacon. The segment then shifts to George Solis, an NBC News Correspondent, standing outdoors in Baltimore, wearing a dark coat, with trees and vehicles in the background, identified by a lower third. Tom introduces George, asking for details about one of the victims, whose body has been discovered. George Solis, addressing Tom, confirms the news and begins to speak about the brother of Maynard Sandoval Swazo.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with NBC News Correspondent George Solis reporting live from Baltimore, dressed in a dark coat over a white shirt, standing outdoors with trees and vehicles in the background. The lower third initially displays 'BREAKING NEWS_' and identifies him as 'GEORGE SOLIS NBC NEWS CORRESPONDENT'. Solis speaks directly to the camera with a serious and clear voice, gesturing with his hands as he reports that a family was called in today, confirming that 'Maynor's body was found there at the wreckage of the bridge.' He explains that more details are expected tomorrow after the body is processed, describing this as the 'update they were fearing the most,' which 'hits heavily' for the family who were awaiting word on their loved one. The text on the lower third then updates to 'JUST IN: BODY FOUND AT THE SITE OF BALTIMORE BRIDGE COLLAPSE' in bold red letters. Solis continues, mentioning that other details are slow to come in, and refers to the victim's brother, who he spoke with last week, confirming the victim leaves behind a young family. He notes the family was praying the body would be found soon and that four family members were still waiting for an update. The scene transitions to a full-screen graphic titled 'Baltimore Bridge Collapse Victims_', set against a blue bokeh background, displaying six portraits: Alejandro Hernandez Fuentes and Dorlian Castillo Cabrera are marked with 'BODY RECOVERED' in red, while Miguel Luna, José López, Maynor Suazo, and Victim 6 are shown with silhouettes or photos, visually confirming the name of the victim Solis mentioned. The graphic remains on screen for several seconds before cutting back to Solis, who continues his report with the same breaking news banner at the bottom, reiterating that the family was called to an undisclosed location this morning and it's now confirmed they found the body, with further processing and details awaited. The environment is bright and clear, suggesting daytime, with light traffic and flags visible in the background.

The graphic displays six portraits: Alejandro Hernandez Fuentes and Dorlian Castillo Cabrera are marked with 'BODY RECOVERED' in red, while Miguel Luna, José López, Maynor Suazo, and Victim 6 are shown with silhouettes or photos. The environment is bright and clear, suggesting daytime, with light traffic and flags visible in the background.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with NBC News Correspondent George Solis reporting live from Baltimore, standing outdoors in a dark coat over a white shirt, with trees and vehicles in the background. The lower third displays 'BREAKING NEWS_ JUST IN: BODY FOUND AT THE SITE OF BALTIMORE BRIDGE COLLAPSE' in bold red and white text, with the NBC News Now logo. Solis, speaking with a serious and informative tone, details the recovery of a body, noting it's unclear if it was found in a vehicle, unlike some previous victims. He mentions working through details with the family and describes it as a "tragic update to an already tragic story," referencing the President's visit and his own meeting with a survivor, Julio Cervantes, who was bedridden after suffering a chest wound but is now in much better spirits. The scene cuts to dramatic aerial footage of the collapsed bridge, showing a large container ship with 'MAERSK' containers colliding with the steel truss structure, causing significant debris in the water, highlighting the scale of the disaster. The footage returns to George Solis, who continues his report, emphasizing the Swazo family's confirmation that the body of their loved one was recovered. The scene then transitions to a split-screen, with Male News Anchor Tom in the studio on the left, wearing a dark suit and light blue checkered shirt, and George Solis on the right. Tom, speaking with a concerned and empathetic tone, acknowledges the family in Honduras who had hoped for the body's discovery and return, finding some "solace." The studio background features 'Hallie Jackson NOW', and the lighting contrasts the natural daylight outdoors with the bright studio environment, all while the breaking news lower third remains consistent.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a male news anchor, dressed in a dark suit and light blue checkered shirt, seated in a modern studio with blue-lit ceilings and marble flooring. His clear, authoritative voice delivers urgent breaking news, reporting on aftershocks being felt across the Northeast after a "wild and rare earthquake" that struck this morning, noting it's the strongest quake in nearly 250 years for the New Jersey epicenter. The large screen behind him initially displays 'Hallie Jackson NOW_' before transitioning to footage of the earthquake event. As the anchor directs viewers to "take a look at this," the broadcast cuts to jarring surveillance footage from a home in Middlesex, NJ, where a hallway shakes violently, objects fall, and a dog runs in panic, accompanied by the distinct sounds of shaking, crashing, a dog's yelp, and a person exclaiming "What happened?". The anchor then explains that the 4.8-magnitude quake was centered just outside Lebanon, NJ, about 50 miles from New York City, with people feeling the shakes from Philly to Boston. A Google Earth map graphic pinpoints the epicenter in Lebanon, NJ, with concentric red circles illustrating seismic waves, reinforcing the anchor's details. He continues, mentioning that more than a dozen people were evacuated due to structural damage, directing attention to on-the-ground footage from Newark, NJ, showing a row of damaged houses with visible structural cracks, yellow caution tape, and emergency personnel, including a worker in a blue hard hat, assessing the damage. The anchor highlights concerns about houses "leaning together" and states that teams are currently checking bridges, roads, buildings, and tunnels. The segment also includes surveillance footage from Boonton Coffee in New Jersey, where patrons and staff react to the shaking, with tables and shelves swaying and people scrambling for safety. Throughout, a breaking news banner at the bottom reads 'NOW: AFTERSHOCKS ROCK NORTHEAST AFTER 4.8-MAGNITUDE EARTHQUAKE,' with the NBC News Now logo.
segments.visual_original_caption: The segment opens with a breaking news alert from NBC News Now, featuring a male news anchor in a dark suit and light blue checkered shirt, seated in a modern studio environment with blue-lit ceilings and marble flooring. The anchor delivers urgent coverage of aftershocks following a 4.8-magnitude earthquake that struck Lebanon, NJ, on Friday, April 5, 2024, at 10:23 AM EDT. The broadcast cuts to surveillance footage from a home in Middlesex, NJ, showing a hallway shaking violently, with objects falling and a dog running in panic. This is followed by a Google Earth map graphic pinpointing the epicenter in Lebanon, NJ, with concentric red circles illustrating the seismic waves. The segment then shifts to on-the-ground footage from Newark, NJ, showing a row of damaged houses with visible structural cracks, yellow caution tape, and emergency personnel assessing the damage. A worker in a blue hard hat and yellow safety vest is seen inspecting a house. The broadcast continues with surveillance footage from Boonton Coffee in New Jersey, where patrons and staff react to the shaking, with tables and shelves swaying and people scrambling for safety. The lower third consistently displays the breaking news banner: 'NOW: AFTERSHOCKS ROCK NORTHEAST AFTER 4.8-MAGNITUDE EARTHQUAKE,' with the NBC News Now logo in the bottom right corner. The overall atmosphere is tense and urgent, emphasizing the widespread impact of the earthquake across the Northeast.

segments.integrated_caption: In a modern news studio with blue-lit ceilings and marble flooring, a Male News Anchor, dressed in a dark suit and light blue checkered shirt, delivers breaking news about a 4.8-magnitude earthquake in New Jersey. He reports no injuries but notes the earthquake triggered ground stops at Newark and JFK airports, causing flight diversions, emphasizing that the East Coast is not accustomed to such events and many older buildings raise structural safety concerns. Behind him, a Large Screen Display initially shows 'Hallie Jackson NOW_' before transitioning to surveillance footage from Boonton Coffee, timestamped 10:23:35 AM to 10:23:56 AM, capturing patrons and staff reacting as tables and shelves sway. This is followed by a blue graphic displaying an FAA tweet (@FAANews) warning of potential air traffic impacts in New York, New Jersey, Philadelphia, and Baltimore, timestamped 10:59 AM - 4/5/2024. The screen then shows aerial footage of Lebanon, NJ, revealing emergency vehicles, including fire trucks and ambulances, responding to visible damage and debris in a residential area. The anchor then introduces meteorologist Michelle Grossman and correspondent Emilie Ikeda (Rehema Ellis), as the broadcast shifts to a split-screen. On the left, Rehema Ellis, an NBC News correspondent (misidentified by a lower third graphic as 'EMILIE IKEDA, NBC NEWS CORRESPONDENT'), reports live from Lebanon, NJ, wearing a navy blue coat and purple scarf, gesturing expressively on a suburban street. On the right, Michelle Grossman, in a light pink blouse and beige pants, stands before a large screen displaying a topographical map of New Jersey with the earthquake epicenter in Lebanon, NJ, marked by concentric red circles, and a graphic overlay stating 'MAGNITUDE 4.8' and '10:23AM EDT FRIDAY, APRIL 5, 2024'. Rehema Ellis confirms she just felt an aftershock, describing it as a 'vibration of the ground,' and highlights officials' warnings for continued aftershocks, noting more than six have occurred. The 'Breaking News Lower Third' consistently reads 'NOW: AFTERSHOCKS ROCK NORTHEAST AFTER 4.8-MAGNITUDE EARTHQUAKE' with the NBC News Now logo.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with NBC News correspondent Rehema Ellis, wearing a navy blue coat, reporting live from Lebanon, NJ at 6:06 PM, standing on a suburban street with houses and passing cars. She speaks with concern, gesturing as she describes the aftermath of the 4.8-magnitude earthquake, noting the public's confusion and the ongoing checks of buildings and infrastructure, before introducing a woman's terrifying experience. During this, surveillance footage from a home in Lebanon, NJ, labeled 'EARLIER TODAY', shows a driveway shaking violently with a 'Nest' logo, followed by overhead security footage from Boonton Coffee in New Jersey, timestamped 10:23:35 AM, where patrons and staff react in panic as tables sway and items fall. The report then cuts to an indoor interview with a woman wearing a beige cap and light cardigan, standing in a home with framed black-and-white photos, who vividly recounts her experience, stating her "whole entire house was shaking... pretty aggressively," with walls trembling, making her think "there was an explosion happening from the basement." The segment concludes with a return to Rehema Ellis, still reporting live at 6:07 PM with pedestrians behind her, who briefly comments on the earthquake being the strongest to hit the East, accompanied by a distinct "ding" and "whoosh" sound effect, while the 'BREAKING NEWS' lower third remains constant, reading 'NOW: AFTERSHOCKS ROCK NORTHEAST AFTER 4.8-MAGNITUDE EARTHQUAKE'.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with NBC News correspondent Rehema Ellis, despite a lower third mislabeling her as Emilie Ikeda, reporting live from Lebanon, NJ at 6:07 PM. She stands on a suburban street in a navy blue coat, detailing the 4.8-magnitude earthquake as the strongest to hit New Jersey in nearly 250 years, addressing the anchor as 'Tom'. The red 'BREAKING NEWS_' banner at the bottom highlights 'AFTERSHOCKS ROCK NORTHEAST'. The scene transitions to a split-screen with the Male News Anchor in the Studio Environment, wearing a dark suit, who discusses the 'online frenzy' and 'meme culture' surrounding the earthquake, specifically referencing the Empire State Building's 'I am fine' and 'not again' tweets. A full-screen graphic then displays these two tweets from the Empire State Building's X account. The broadcast returns to the split-screen, then to Rehema Ellis alone at 6:08 PM, who, with expressive hand gestures, agrees on the 'true meme culture fashion' of the online reactions. She explains that the lack of reported injuries and widespread damage allows people to have 'a little bit more fun' on social media, citing examples like 'I have survived the New York City earthquake' t-shirts, jokes about a single chair being knocked over, and Californians' potential reactions. A full-screen graphic then illustrates these points, showing a 'I SURVIVED THE NYC EARTHQUAKE' t-shirt, a tweet about Californians, and a 'WE WILL REBUILD' post over a damaged patio. Rehema Ellis concludes her report, emphasizing the Northeast's unfamiliarity with such seismic events.
segments.visual_original_caption: The segment opens with Rehema Ellis, an NBC News correspondent, reporting live from Lebanon, NJ at 6:07 PM, standing on a suburban street with houses and pedestrians in the background, wearing a navy blue coat over a beige turtleneck. The red 'BREAKING NEWS_' banner at the bottom reads 'NOW: AFTERSHOCKS ROCK NORTHEAST AFTER 4.8-MAGNITUDE EARTHQUAKE'. The scene transitions to a split-screen with the Male News Anchor in the Studio Environment, wearing a dark suit and light blue checkered shirt, gesturing as he speaks. The studio background displays 'Hallie Jackson NOW_' on a large screen. The broadcast then cuts to a full-screen graphic showing a tweet from the Empire State Building (@EmpireStateBldg) with the text 'I AM FINE' timestamped 10:24 AM - 4/5/2024, followed by another tweet from the same account reading 'NOT AGAIN' at 5:59 PM - 4/5/2024, both displayed on a blue background with the 'X' logo. The segment returns to the split-screen with the anchor and Rehema Ellis, then back to Rehema Ellis alone, now at 6:08 PM, continuing her report with expressive hand gestures. The broadcast then displays a full-screen graphic with three social media posts: one showing a white t-shirt with 'I SURVIVED THE NYC EARTHQUAKE' printed on it, another with the text 'can you imagine how humiliating this would be for us if the californians were awake', and a third with 'WE WILL REBUILD' over an image of a damaged patio set, all timestamped April 5, 2024. The segment concludes with Rehema Ellis continuing her report, emphasizing the ongoing aftershocks and public reactions.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment begins at 6:08 PM with NBC News correspondent Emily (visually identified as Rehema Ellis, though the anchor addresses her as Emily) reporting live from Lebanon, NJ. She stands on a suburban street with houses and passing cars, wearing a navy blue coat over a beige turtleneck, and speaks with concern, gesturing as she describes the aftershocks following a 4.8-magnitude earthquake, stating, "it was, in the moment, really scary and confusing as the ground rocked for seconds." The scene transitions to a split-screen where the Male News Anchor, in a dark suit and light blue checkered shirt, is seated in the Studio Environment, speaking seriously and gesturing toward a Large Screen Display. He adds context, saying, "Yeah, and let's keep in mind, this is also an area that was hit on 9/11. So all of that's still very real for people in the New York area. Emily, thank you very much." The screen initially shows the 'Hallie Jackson NOW_' graphic before switching to an Earthquake Map Display of the Northeast, showing the epicenter near Whitehouse Station, NJ, with concentric red circles indicating seismic waves, labeled 'MAGNITUDE 4.8' and '10:23 AM ET'. The anchor continues, introducing "meteorologist Michelle Grossman," and discussing how people "really felt this and they were, they were really terrified," questioning why the quake "doesn't quite stack up to the magnitudes of the quakes out there in California." The lower third consistently reads 'BREAKING NEWS_ NOW: AFTERSHOCKS ROCK NORTHEAST AFTER 4.8-MAGNITUDE EARTHQUAKE'. The scene then shifts to Michelle Grossman, NBC News Meteorologist, standing in front of the same Earthquake Map Display in the studio. Wearing a light pink blouse and beige pants, she uses hand gestures to explain the earthquake’s reach and impact, responding, "Yeah, we can almost hear it, right? Uh, well, we can tell them a few things because we are feeling it different than they would in California. There's three major reasons... One of the major reasons is we have really dense rock as compared to the West. We have the Appalachian Mountains, which are way older than the Sierra Nevada." The studio environment features blue-lit walls and a large screen, with the time updating to 6:09 PM in Lebanon, NJ, as the anchor and meteorologist interact with the map, emphasizing the earthquake’s significance and geographic spread.

segments.integrated_caption: NBC News meteorologist Michelle Grossman, dressed in a light pink blouse and beige pants, stands in a blue-lit studio, expertly explaining earthquake data while holding a remote. She gestures towards a large screen initially displaying a satellite map of the Northeast, pinpointing a 4.8-magnitude earthquake epicenter near Whitehouse Station, NJ, at 10:23 AM ET. A prominent red 'BREAKING NEWS' banner at the bottom highlights the event as the "biggest earthquake in over a decade." Michelle articulates that the Northeast's older, packed mountains and stronger crust easily transmit earthquake energy, making it feel stronger and spread wider than in California. As she speaks, the map transitions to a topographical view with color-coded intensity zones—'WEAK', 'LIGHT', 'MODERATE', and 'STRONG'—radiating from the epicenter. She points to locations like New York, Trenton, and Allentown, emphasizing how the tremors can reach 300 miles. She further explains that the earthquake's shallow depth contributes to its intensity, as energy goes directly to the surface. Michelle also contrasts building codes, mentioning a conversation with climate specialist Chase Kane about California's strict codes that allow buildings to sway, a feature not present in the Northeast. The USGS is credited as the source for the map, and the NBC News Now logo is visible, with global time indicators changing from 'LEBANON, NJ 6:09 PM' to 'JERUSALEM 1:09 AM'.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with NBC News meteorologist Michelle Grossman, dressed in a light pink blouse and beige pants, standing before a large screen displaying a topographical map titled 'NORTHEAST EARTHQUAKE' from USGS, which shows color-coded intensity zones and labeled cities. She gestures with her hands, holding a small black device, as she explains the earthquake's impact, noting how people felt their buildings shaking and comparing it to past events in 2011 and the 80s. She emphasizes that for many, this 4.8-magnitude earthquake is the third strongest in New Jersey since the 1700s, meaning they may never feel such an event again. The lower third banner initially reads 'BREAKING NEWS: 4.8-MAGNITUDE EARTHQUAKE RATTLES THE NORTHEAST WITH BIGGEST EARTHQUAKE IN OVER A DECADE.' Michelle then shifts to discussing the high chances for aftershocks, citing the USGS, and advises viewers to "drop, cover, and hold," while the banner updates to 'NOW: AFTERSHOCKS ROCK NORTHEAST AFTER 4.8-MAGNITUDE EARTHQUAKE.' She clarifies that a stronger aftershock is statistically unlikely but a 48% chance exists for a three-magnitude or less. The scene then transitions to a split-screen, with a Male News Anchor, wearing a dark suit and light blue checkered shirt, appearing on the left in a blue-lit studio environment. He speaks animatedly, using hand gestures, to elaborate on New York City's "unbelievably strong bedrock" and how that affects the spread of shaking, while Michelle Grossman remains on the right, still in front of the earthquake map. The NBC News Now logo is visible throughout, reinforcing the urgent and informative atmosphere.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens in a modern news studio with a blue-lit background, where a Male News Anchor, dressed in a dark suit and light blue checkered shirt, delivers breaking news in a serious tone. His voice is heard discussing the 4.8-magnitude earthquake in the Northeast, engaging in a brief exchange about "nerdy stuff" before transitioning to the situation overseas. Beside him, a Large Screen Display initially shows a detailed Earthquake Map Display with color-coded intensity zones and labeled cities like Morristown and New York, sourced from USGS, while a Breaking News Lower Third reads 'NOW: AFTERSHOCKS ROCK NORTHEAST AFTER 4.8-MAGNITUDE EARTHQUAKE'. The scene then transitions to a full-screen blue graphic with 'Hallie Jackson NOW_' before shifting to a dramatic aerial view of a mountainous disaster zone under the 'RACE TO RESCUE' banner. This footage, credited to the Pingtung Fire Department and Taiwan National Fire Agency, shows rescue teams in helmets and yellow gear navigating through debris and unstable terrain in Taiwan, as the anchor's voice describes rescuers facing threats of landslides and falling rocks while trying to save people trapped after a 7.4-magnitude earthquake off Taiwan's eastern coast, mentioning a rescuer giving an injured man a piggyback ride and crews finding more bodies. The screen then splits, showing a severely tilted building in an urban area (TVBS footage) on the left and rescue workers in red and yellow uniforms (Taiwan Fire Department) searching through rubble on the right, with a headline stating 'Taiwan's 7.4-Magnitude Earthquake_ As of April 5: At least 12 people dead, 1,100+ hurt; 600+ trapped & at least 13 missing', which the anchor narrates, detailing the rising casualty count. The segment concludes with Janis Mackey Frayer, NBC News International Correspondent, reporting live from Hualien, Taiwan, at night, wearing a dark button-up shirt, standing in front of damaged buildings and emergency activity, her voice heard beginning her report from Toroko Gorge, with the lower third identifying her and the location. The NBC News Now branding is visible throughout.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with rescue teams in high-visibility vests and helmets navigating a treacherous mountainous debris field in Taiwan, carrying a victim on a stretcher through unstable terrain, as indicated by the on-screen text 'Fire Bureau of Taichung City Government' and 'RESCUE TEAMS COMB THROUGH MOUNTAINS IN TAIWAN TO FIND TRAPPED QUAKE VICTIMS'. Janis Mackey Frayer, NBC News International Correspondent, reports live from Hualien, Taiwan, at night, standing in front of damaged buildings and emergency activity, explaining that rescue teams have been working for days to reach hundreds of people stranded in a national park in the mountains, cut off by landslides. The scene transitions to a red rescue helicopter marked 'NA-713' on a tarmac, where personnel in yellow and red gear rush to load supplies, footage credited to the 'Taiwan National Fire Agency', illustrating the efforts to take food and water to those stranded until they can be airlifted out, though several people are still reported missing. Janis emphasizes the urgency, stating that the 72-hour window rescue officials talk about maximizing is now beginning to close, with rain in the forecast and over 400 aftershocks posing a continuous challenge, forcing rescue teams to retreat for safety. The human toll is highlighted by an injured man on a stretcher, wearing glasses and a face mask, being treated by medical personnel, with a police officer and rescue workers attending to him. Dramatic shots from 'TVBS' then show a severely tilted building in an urban area with debris and bystanders, followed by surveillance footage from 'cti 中天新聞' inside a restaurant, capturing the ceiling and lights swaying violently during the quake, with people reacting in panic. The final clip, surveillance from 'Ma Cherie Maternity Centre', shows staff in pink uniforms and masks tending to newborns in incubators as the room shakes, with a red '平安' (peace/safety) decoration on the wall and a timestamp of 24/04/03 07:58:59, underscoring the vulnerability of medical facilities. Throughout, the NBC News Now branding and the headline 'RESCUE TEAMS COMB THROUGH MOUNTAINS IN TAIWAN TO FIND TRAPPED QUAKE VICTIMS' remain consistent in the lower third.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with surveillance footage from Taiwan's Ma Cherie Maternity Centre, showing staff in pink uniforms and masks tending to newborns in incubators as the room violently shakes during an earthquake, a red '平安' (peace/safety) decoration visible on the wall. NBC News International Correspondent Janis Mackey Frayer reports live from Hualien, Taiwan, at night, standing before damaged buildings and emergency activity, gesturing as she speaks about Taiwan's earthquake preparedness, including cell phone alerts, public awareness, and strict building codes that have led to reinforced structures. She highlights the tilting building behind her, noting it's been on the verge of collapse for days and officials have decided to demolish it. A male studio anchor then introduces Janis Mackey Frayer, before transitioning to the NBC News Now studio where a large screen displays 'TAIWAN ISRAEL MARKETS ECLIPSE' and 'THE FIVE THINGS'. The anchor reports that the Israeli military has dismissed two senior officers and released a new report on 'serious errors and violations' that led to a deadly Israeli attack, killing seven aid workers in Gaza. The visual shifts to Deir Al-Balah, Gaza, showing a burnt-out white SUV with its doors open and charred interior, surrounded by debris, as the anchor continues, stating that World Central Kitchen is demanding an independent probe into those strikes, with a lower third confirming 'IDF DISMISSES TWO SENIOR OFFICERS OVER KILLING OF SEVEN AID WORKERS IN GAZA'.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with harrowing footage from Deir Al-Balah, Gaza, showing a destroyed white SUV, branded 'World Central Kitchen', its doors open and interior charred, surrounded by debris and bystanders, under a lower third banner reading 'IDF DISMISSES TWO SENIOR OFFICERS OVER KILLING OF SEVEN AID WORKERS IN GAZA'. The scene shifts to chaotic street views in Gaza, where civilians navigate through rubble, some carrying goods, while others gather around makeshift stalls under colorful umbrellas, highlighting the humanitarian crisis. The footage then cuts to a crowded street with people, including women in hijabs and children, moving through the devastated urban landscape. The segment transitions to a split-screen studio interview where a Male News Anchor, in a dark suit and light blue checkered shirt, speaks from the Studio Environment, with a large screen behind him displaying 'Hallie Jackson NOW_'. He reports that Minister Benjamin Netanyahu calls the killings unintentional and that the Biden administration is offering cautious optimism over Israel's new steps to allow more aid into Gaza, following President Biden's ultimatum to Netanyahu. He then introduces NBC News International Correspondent Hala Gorani, asking for more information on how the World Central Kitchen is responding to the report and the devastating loss of seven individuals. On the right, Hala Gorani, appearing live from Jerusalem at 1:15 AM, wearing a beige trench coat over a dark top with a gold necklace, stands before a blurred cityscape at night. She responds, stating that the World Central Kitchen is demanding a fully independent probe into the attack that left seven employees dead, and notes that the IDF spokesperson, Daniel Hagari, added to a written statement taking full responsibility for the attack. Throughout, the 'ISRAEL-HAMAS WAR' tag and NBC News Now branding remain visible, as the video returns to the destroyed vehicle in Gaza, showing it from multiple angles with people inspecting the wreckage, reinforcing the gravity of the incident.

She reports that the World Central Kitchen is demanding a fully independent probe into the attack that killed seven of its workers, and that the IDF spokesperson, Daniel Hagari, has taken full responsibility for the attack.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of a destroyed white SUV, charred and with open doors, amidst debris in Deir Al-Balah, Gaza, set against palm trees and a concrete wall. A lower third banner reads 'IDF DISMISSES TWO SENIOR OFFICERS OVER KILLING OF SEVEN AID WORKERS IN GAZA' under the 'ISRAEL-HAMAS WAR' tag. A text overlay on the wreckage then appears, stating 'IDF ON DEADLY STRIKES KILLING SEVEN AID WORKERS' with quotes highlighting 'The strikes on the three vehicles were carried out in serious violation of the commands...' and '... a grave mistake stemming from a serious failure due to a mistaken identification, errors in decision-making...'. The scene transitions to NBC News International Correspondent Hala Gorani, standing outdoors in Jerusalem at night, wearing a beige trench coat over a dark top with a gold necklace, reporting live with a stone wall and blurred city lights behind her. With a composed demeanor, she explains that "serious errors were committed," describing it as a "terrible chain of errors" that "should not have happened," and stating that "the Israeli military is fully responsible." She highlights that for World Central Kitchen and "four other aid organizations," this speaks to a "wider problem," noting that the "seven people who died on April 1st are not the only humanitarians who've lost their lives since October 7th." She adds that the UN Secretary General mentioned "almost 200 humanitarians have been killed in strikes and in the violence since October 7th," which is "in addition... to the tremendous loss of civilian life." Gorani concludes that "more needs to be done, not just to protect humanitarian workers, but to get that desperately needed aid into the Gaza Strip in a much more seamless fashion," and that "the Israeli government has to do more to facilitate that." The scene then shifts to a split-screen, with a male news anchor in a dark suit and light blue checkered shirt in the studio on the left, displaying 'Hallie Jackson NOW_' on a large screen behind him, while Hala Gorani continues her report on the right. The lower third updates to 'ISRAEL OPENS MORE AID ROUTES AFTER PRESIDENT BIDEN GAVE NETANYAHU ULTIMATUM'. The male news anchor then asks, "And Hala, give us an update on the..."

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a split-screen view, showing a Male News Anchor in a dark suit and light blue checkered shirt, seated in a Studio Environment with a 'Hallie Jackson NOW_' screen behind him, asking with a serious expression, "aid that is supposed to be flowing into Gaza with famine striking much of that region and the death toll is surpassing 30,000. How long could that aid actually last?" On the right, NBC News International Correspondent Hala Gorani, wearing a beige trench coat over a dark top with a gold necklace, reports live from Jerusalem at night, with a stone wall and blurred city lights behind her. A lower third banner reads 'ISRAEL OPENS MORE AID ROUTES AFTER PRES. BIDEN GAVE NETANYAHU ULTIMATUM' under the 'ISRAEL-HAMAS WAR' tag. The scene transitions to a full-screen view of Hala Gorani, with 'JERUSALEM 1:16 AM' in the top left, as she responds, "Right, so we're not sure exactly when what the Israeli government has promised will happen will materialize, that the Erez crossing in the north is meant to reopen. It's been closed since October 7th, that the Ashdod Port is meant to be used. These are according to the statements from the Israeli government over the last 24 hours following that what was described as a tense phone call between President Biden and Benjamin Netanyahu, the Israeli Prime Minister." The camera remains steady on her before cutting to a graphic titled 'NEW STEPS ISRAEL SAYS IT WILL TAKE TO LET MORE AID INTO GAZA,' which lists the temporary reopening of the Erez Crossing, allowing sea aid through the Israeli port, and increasing aid shipments from Jordan, each with small illustrative images over a faint map background. The video returns to Hala Gorani, who continues her report with a composed demeanor, emphasizing, "But look, in the past, the Israeli government has promised, you'll remember a few weeks ago to flood the north with aid, that didn't happen. The Secretary of State Anthony Blinken is also saying that, you know, we need to see on the ground exactly what transpires before making a judgment as to whether or not the Israeli government..." The city lights softly glow in the background, maintaining a somber nighttime tone, with the NBC News Now logo visible in the lower right corner throughout.

The graphic is displayed over a faint map background. The video returns to Hala Gorani, who continues her report with a composed demeanor, emphasizing the developments in the humanitarian situation. The lighting remains consistent, with the nighttime setting providing a somber tone to the report. The NBC News Now logo is visible in the lower right corner throughout.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with U.S. Secretary of State Antony Blinken, in a dark suit and tie, speaking with a serious expression before a U.S. government aircraft in Brussels. He states that Israel is taking full responsibility for an incident and taking steps to hold those responsible accountable, emphasizing the need to prioritize civilian protection in military operations against Hamas. A lower third identifies him and the context: 'ISRAEL OPENS MORE AID ROUTES AFTER PRES. BIDEN GAVE NETANYAHU ULTIMATUM.' The scene transitions to NBC News International Correspondent Hala Gorani, reporting live from Jerusalem at 1:18 AM, wearing a beige trench coat over a dark top with a gold necklace, standing before a stone wall with blurred city lights. She discusses the ongoing wait to see when and how much aid, including trucks and vehicles, will be allowed into the region. The video then cuts to a split-screen interview, showing a Male News Anchor in a dark suit and light blue checkered shirt in a studio environment with a 'Hallie Jackson NOW_' screen behind him, thanking Hala Gorani for her expertise and intimate knowledge of the situation. The scene shifts to a studio graphic with a blue archway, displaying a black-and-white image of a destroyed vehicle with 'SENIOR OFFICERS DISMISSED' and 'World Central Kitchen' visible, under the headline 'TAIWAN ISRAEL MARKETS ECLIPSE THE FIVE THINGS.' This transitions to a black-and-white image of a stock exchange floor with 'IN THE GREEN' displayed, followed by a large screen in the studio showing 'U.S. Stock Market at Close_' with green arrows and positive gains for the DOW (+307.06), S&P 500 (+57.13), and NASDAQ (+199.44). The Male News Anchor discusses Wall Street, noting the stock markets closed in the green after a 'stronger than expected,' 'booming,' and 'eye-popping' March jobs report, before mentioning a check of the boards, specifically the Dow. The studio environment features blue-lit backgrounds, consistent branding, and the NBC News Now logo in the lower right corner throughout.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a large news studio screen displaying positive 'U.S. Stock Market at Close_' figures, showing the DOW up 307.06, S&P 500 up 57.13, and NASDAQ up 199.44. A Male News Anchor immediately confirms these gains, noting the market is "up 300 points on a Friday," while also mentioning the Dow's "worst week in 2024" and the S&P and NASDAQ being "up more than 1% today." The visuals then transition to outdoor shots of 'HELP WANTED' signs, including one for 'Rose Paving Co.' and another for 'Bales Cash' offering $45,000, all beneath a prominent blue banner announcing 'U.S. ADDS 303,000 JOBS IN MARCH, IN STRONGER-THAN-EXPECTED REPORT' and an 'INFLATION IN AMERICA' tag. The anchor elaborates, stating that experts predicted 200,000 new jobs, but a "stunner" of 303,000 jobs were added, "50% more than what economists had expected," marking the "39th straight month of job growth in America." He describes it as a "very good sign" contrasting with a year ago when a recession was "imminent." The scene briefly shows a retail checkout with a customer using a credit card and a cashier handling cash, illustrating consumer activity. The segment then shifts to a split-screen studio setup, with the Male News Anchor on the left and NBC News Senior Business Correspondent Christine Romans on the right, standing beside a large screen titled 'JOBS IN AMERICA'. The anchor introduces her, saying "NBC's Christine Romans is of course breaking it all down for us." The screen behind Romans displays a 'Jobs Gained Per Month' bar graph, with the March bar highlighted in red, as she gestures towards it. The anchor then poses a question to her, acknowledging the "good news" of more jobs and rising wages, but questioning if it's "bad news" if the economy is "still hot," potentially delaying interest rate cuts. Christine Romans responds, asking, "Well, I mean, look, Tom, do you cut interest rates when a job market looks like this?"
segments.visual_original_caption: The video opens with a large screen in a news studio displaying the 'U.S. Stock Market at Close_' with green upward arrows and positive gains for the DOW (+307.06), S&P 500 (+57.13), and NASDAQ (+199.44), indicating a strong market close. The scene transitions to outdoor shots of 'HELP WANTED' signs on businesses, including one for 'Rose Paving Co.' seeking an Asphalt Luteman and Pavebox Operator, and another for 'Bales Cash' offering $45,000, all under a blue banner reading 'U.S. ADDS 303,000 JOBS IN MARCH, IN STRONGER-THAN-EXPECTED REPORT' and a tag 'INFLATION IN AMERICA'. The footage then cuts to a retail checkout, showing a customer inserting a credit card into a payment terminal and a cashier handling cash, emphasizing consumer activity. The segment shifts to a split-screen studio setup where the Male News Anchor, in a dark suit and light blue checkered shirt, speaks from the left, while Christine Romans, NBC News Senior Business Correspondent, stands on the right beside a large screen titled 'JOBS IN AMERICA'. The screen transitions to a bar graph labeled 'Jobs Gained Per Month' (Bureau of Labor Statistics), showing monthly job gains in thousands from March to February, with the March bar highlighted in red. Christine Romans gestures toward the graph as she explains the data, with the lower third identifying her and the ongoing headline about the 303,000 jobs added in March.

segments.integrated_caption: NBC News Senior Business Correspondent Christine Romans, dressed in a black blazer over a white top with a necklace and bracelets, stands in a blue-lit studio environment, gesturing expressively as she delivers an economic report. She begins by discussing the robust job market, stating, "that does not look like it needs a little bit of help here. I mean, look at the job market just this year has actually accelerated." As she speaks, a bar graph titled 'Jobs Gained Per Month' is displayed on a large screen, showing monthly job gains with the March bar highlighted in red, accompanied by the on-screen text 'U.S. ADDS 303,000 JOBS IN MARCH, IN STRONGER-THAN-EXPECTED REPORT'. Christine then transitions to discussing the unemployment rate, pointing to a line graph titled 'Unemployment Rate' that depicts a decline to 3.8% in March, with the lower third text updating to 'UNEMPLOYMENT RATE FALLS TO 3.8 PERCENT IN MARCH, FROM 3.9 PERCENT IN FEBRUARY'. She highlights the sustained low unemployment, noting, "28 months below 4% for the jobless rate. That hasn't happened since the 1960s." She continues to analyze the job market's resilience, stating it "has absorbed all of those rate hikes and is still strong," before discussing wage growth at 4.1%, which she notes is "a little bit cooler than the month before," potentially offering comfort to the Fed. Throughout the segment, the 'INFLATION IN AMERICA' tag remains visible in the lower left corner, and the NBC News Now logo is in the lower right.

The lower third text updates to 'UNEMPLOYMENT RATE FALLS TO 3.8 PERCENT IN MARCH, FROM 3.9 PERCENT IN FEBRUARY'. Throughout, the 'INFLATION IN AMERICA' tag remains in the lower left corner, and the NBC News Now logo is visible in the lower right. Christine Romans points to the graphs, emphasizing key data points, particularly the March figures, while maintaining a professional and informative demeanor.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a split-screen in a modern, brightly lit news studio. On the left, the Male News Anchor, dressed in a dark suit and light blue checkered shirt, speaks with a serious expression, gesturing with his right hand. On the right, Christine Romans, the NBC News Senior Business Correspondent, stands in a black blazer over a white top with a necklace and bracelets, gesturing toward a large screen displaying a line graph titled 'Unemployment Rate' from the Bureau of Labor Statistics, showing data from 2010 to 2024 with a sharp 2020 spike and a decline to 3.8% in March. The lower third of the screen features a blue banner stating 'UNEMPLOYMENT RATE FALLS TO 3.8 PERCENT IN MARCH, FROM 3.9 PERCENT IN FEBRUARY' and an 'INFLATION IN AMERICA' tag. The NBC News Now logo is visible in the lower right. Christine begins by highlighting the importance of the economic data, to which the Male News Anchor interjects, noting that despite wage increases, grocery inflation (up 30% over two years) means wages aren't keeping up. Christine acknowledges this, explaining that while wages are now steadily increasing and inflation tapering, there are 'three years of inflation scars' that will take time to heal. As the camera gradually zooms in on her, emphasizing her hand movements pointing to the graph's key points, she elaborates on recent polls, like the University of Michigan poll, showing that people 55 and older feel better about the economy due to exposure to record high stock and home prices, whereas younger individuals still face significant costs and troubles, which she suggests is a reason for the Biden administration's aggressive pursuit of student loan forgiveness.

Christine, wearing a black blazer over a white top with a necklace and bracelets, gestures toward the graph as she explains the data. The studio environment is brightly lit with blue-lit background panels, and the NBC News Now logo is visible in the lower right corner. The overall atmosphere is professional and informative, focusing on economic trends.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a split-screen in the Studio Environment, where the Male News Anchor, in a dark suit, discusses economic data with Christine Romans, who stands beside a large screen displaying a declining 'Unemployment Rate' graph, showing 3.8% in March. The Male News Anchor thanks Christine Romans for her expertise. The scene then transitions to a full-screen 'Hallie Jackson NOW_' logo, followed by a large studio screen showing 'TAIWAN ISRAEL MARKETS ECLIPSE' and 'THE FIVE THINGS', then a black-and-white image of a destroyed vehicle with 'SENIOR OFFICERS DISMISSED' and 'World Central Kitchen'. The Male News Anchor's voice continues, emphasizing that 'the clock is ticking' to get eclipse glasses and make plans for the 'once in a generation' total solar eclipse coming to America, which will create complete darkness for a few minutes. Dramatic footage of a solar eclipse appears, with a lower third stating 'TOURISTS FLOCK TO LOCATIONS ALONG THE SOLAR ECLIPSE PATH OF TOTALITY', followed by a US map highlighting the 'ECLIPSE PATH OF TOTALITY' in yellow, marking cities like Buffalo and Dallas. The Male News Anchor explains this path runs through over a dozen states, turning them into huge tourist attractions. The broadcast then cuts to a split-screen, with the Male News Anchor on the left and NBC News Correspondent Priscilla Thompson, in a yellow sleeveless top, reporting live from Grapevine, TX, at 5:22 PM. She gestures expressively against a bustling street background with bartenders preparing drinks. The Male News Anchor introduces Priscilla, asking how Dallas is preparing, to which Priscilla Thompson responds, confirming she's 'out on the streets with the people'.
segments.visual_original_caption: The segment begins with a split-screen view in the Studio Environment, where the Male News Anchor, dressed in a dark suit and light blue checkered shirt, discusses economic data, specifically the unemployment rate, while Christine Romans, in a black blazer over a white top, stands beside a large screen displaying a line graph titled 'Unemployment Rate' showing a decline to 3.8% in March. The scene transitions to a full-screen graphic of the 'Hallie Jackson NOW_' logo, followed by a large screen in the studio showing a blue background with gold text reading 'TAIWAN ISRAEL MARKETS ECLIPSE' and 'THE FIVE THINGS'. This shifts to a black-and-white image of a destroyed vehicle with the text 'SENIOR OFFICERS DISMISSED' and 'World Central Kitchen' visible on the wreckage. The broadcast then cuts to dramatic footage of a solar eclipse, with the sun partially obscured, accompanied by the lower third text 'TOURISTS FLOCK TO LOCATIONS ALONG THE SOLAR ECLIPSE PATH OF TOTALITY'. A map of the United States appears, highlighting the 'ECLIPSE PATH OF TOTALITY' in yellow, with marked cities including Buffalo, NY, Cleveland, OH, Indianapolis, IN, and Dallas, TX. The scene transitions to a split-screen with the Male News Anchor on the left and Priscilla Thompson, an NBC News Correspondent in a yellow sleeveless top, reporting live from Grapevine, TX, at 5:22 PM. She gestures expressively as she speaks, with a bustling street and parked cars in the background, while bartenders prepare drinks behind her. The lower third identifies her as 'PRISCILLA THOMPSON, NBC NEWS CORRESPONDENT' and displays a 'TOTAL ECLIPSE' graphic with moon phases.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens at 5:22 PM with NBC News Correspondent Priscilla Thompson, in a bright yellow sleeveless top, reporting live from a bustling brick-paved sidewalk in Grapevine, TX, with cars and pedestrians moving in the background. Her clear voice explains that downtown streets were already busy with tourists trickling in, anticipating over a million visitors to Texas for the upcoming solar eclipse, as indicated by the 'TOTAL ECLIPSE' graphic and lower third stating 'TOURISTS FLOCK TO LOCATIONS ALONG THE SOLAR ECLIPSE PATH OF TOTALITY'. The scene briefly cuts to a storefront window displaying eclipse-themed merchandise, including a black t-shirt for 'SOLAR ECLIPSE APRIL 08 2024' and another with 'ECLIPSE 2024' in colorful lettering, followed by a close-up of the 'GRAPEVINE TEXAS' water tower. Priscilla's narration continues, introducing an interview with Elizabeth Schrack from the Grapevine Convention & Visitors Bureau. They are seen standing on the sidewalk, with Elizabeth wearing a black t-shirt featuring a yellow eclipse graphic. Priscilla's voice asks about the economic boon, and Elizabeth's clear voice enthusiastically responds, detailing expectations of thousands of visitors from across the country coming to shop, eat at local restaurants, and have fun. The segment concludes at 5:23 PM with Priscilla back on camera, the busy street still visible behind her, as she highlights the 83 Texas counties in the path of totality and notes that rentals from Texas to Maine are nearly completely sold out, emphasizing the significant economic impact.

The lower third graphic reads 'TOTAL ECLIPSE' and 'TOURISTS FLOCK TO LOCATIONS ALONG THE SOLAR ECLIPSE PATH OF TOTALITY'. The scene cuts to a storefront window displaying eclipse-themed merchandise, including a black t-shirt with 'SOLAR ECLIPSE APRIL 08 2024' and a stylized sun and moon design. A close-up shows a large white water tower with 'GRAPEVINE TEXAS' and a grape logo. The video then returns to the storefront, highlighting another t-shirt with 'ECLIPSE 2024' in colorful, playful lettering. Priscilla interviews Elizabeth Schrack from the Grapevine Convention & Visitors Bureau, who wears a black t-shirt with a yellow eclipse graphic. They stand on the sidewalk, gesturing as they discuss the influx of visitors. The segment concludes with Priscilla back on camera, continuing her report at 5:23 PM, with the same busy street and pedestrians in the background, emphasizing the town’s preparation and excitement for the upcoming eclipse event.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with NBC News correspondent Priscilla Thompson, wearing a bright yellow sleeveless top, reporting live from Grapevine, TX, at 5:23 PM. She stands on a brick sidewalk in front of a bustling street, gesturing expressively as she discusses the influx of tourists along the solar eclipse path of totality, noting the "boom for small towns" and larger cities like Dallas, and addressing the anchor as "Tom." The lower third displays 'TOTAL ECLIPSE' and 'TOURISTS FLOCK TO LOCATIONS ALONG THE SOLAR ECLIPSE PATH OF TOTALITY'. The scene cuts to a split-screen where the Male News Anchor, identified as Tom, is in the Studio Environment, seated in front of a large screen displaying 'Hallie Jackson NOW_'. He gestures with his hand, remarking on the "ominous" sound of the "path of totality" and asking what people should keep in mind even if they're not in the full path. Priscilla responds, explaining that even New York City will see some of the eclipse, and stressing the importance of certified glasses. She holds up a pair of white eclipse glasses with purple lenses, showing them to the camera and pointing to the 'ISO' certification on the side, calling them "the real deal." She adds that they can be picked up at places like Target, Walmart, and local libraries. A graphic then appears with the title 'WHERE TO BUY ECLIPSE GLASSES', listing approved sellers, major retailers, and local libraries, reinforcing Priscilla's points. The split-screen returns, with Tom touching his chin thoughtfully as he asks Priscilla to "zoom in to that little logo" on the glasses so people can identify "what should they be looking for" to ensure their glasses are "legit." Priscilla holds the glasses closer to the camera, emphasizing the ISO label, while cars pass by and storefronts with red and green awnings are visible in the bright daylight background. The NBC News Now logo is consistently visible in the lower right corner.

The lower third displays 'TOTAL ECLIPSE' and 'TOURISTS FLOCK TO LOCATIONS ALONG THE SOLAR ECLIPSE PATH OF TOTALITY'. The scene cuts to a split-screen with the Male News Anchor in the Studio Environment, seated in front of a large screen displaying 'Hallie Jackson NOW_', engaging with Priscilla. He gestures with his hand as he speaks, while Priscilla listens and responds. The video returns to Priscilla, who now holds up a pair of white eclipse glasses with purple lenses, showing them to the camera and pointing to the 'ISO' certification on the side. She continues to explain their importance. A graphic then appears with the title 'WHERE TO BUY ECLIPSE GLASSES', listing approved sellers, major retailers like Walmart and Target, and local libraries. The split-screen returns, with the Male News Anchor touching his chin thoughtfully as Priscilla holds the glasses closer to the camera, emphasizing the ISO label. The background remains consistent with cars passing by and storefronts with red and green awnings, under bright daylight. The NBC News Now logo is visible in the lower right corner throughout.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a split-screen interview in a modern news studio, where the Male News Anchor, dressed in a dark suit, engages with NBC News Correspondent Priscilla Thompson, who is in a bright yellow sleeveless top and holding white eclipse glasses. The anchor warns about 'fraudulent like glasses out there,' to which Priscilla, identified as being 'on the ground in Dallas,' responds, 'Yeah, we'd be aware of that.' The studio's large screen displays 'Hallie Jackson NOW_' before transitioning to a blue graphic for 'THE FIVE THINGS'. A 'COMING UP' preview follows, with the Male News Anchor announcing 'the new trend at airports, kind of freaking out the TSA,' shown with a woman at an airport security checkpoint. He then highlights 'LeBron James's son,' Bronny James, in a USC jersey, who 'wants his own shot at the NBA' and is 'declaring for the draft after his rough freshman year,' all set to background music. After a brief blue screen with 'Hallie Jackson NOW_', the Male News Anchor introduces 'The Five Things' segment. The first story features a woman in an orange prison jumpsuit, identified by a lower third as 'WOMAN CONVICTED OF KILLING FRIEND WITH EYE DROPS SENTENCED TO LIFE IN PRISON'. The anchor narrates that she was convicted of 'killing and poisoning her friend with eye drops' and 'motivated by greed,' with scenes of people hugging and celebrating. The second story shows various 'FOR SALE' signs in front of suburban homes, with a lower third reading 'JUSTICE DEPARTMENT TO REOPEN INQUIRY INTO NATIONAL ASSOCIATION OF REALTORS'. The anchor explains that a federal appeals court says the Justice Department 'can reopen its investigation into the National Association of Realtors policies.'

The scene transitions to a blue screen with 'Hallie Jackson NOW_' before moving to 'The Five Things' segment. The second story shows a 'FOR SALE' sign in front of a house, with the lower third reading 'JUSTICE DEPARTMENT TO REOPEN INQUIRY INTO NATIONAL ASSOCIATION OF REALTORS'. The footage includes various 'FOR SALE' signs from different real estate agencies, such as 'SAMSON PROPERTIES' and 'THE LOCAL COMPASS', set against suburban homes under clear skies.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with exterior shots of suburban homes, each prominently displaying 'FOR SALE' signs from various real estate agencies like 'Atlanta Intown' and 'TTR Sotheby's International Realty', under clear skies. The accompanying audio discusses a group (implied to be the National Association of Realtors, as indicated by the lower third graphic 'JUSTICE DEPARTMENT TO REOPEN INQUIRY INTO NATIONAL ASSOCIATION OF REALTORS') that settled government anti-trust claims and a huge anti-trust lawsuit on behalf of home sellers. The scene then transitions to a busy airport security checkpoint, where travelers place items on conveyor belts and walk through metal detectors, monitored by TSA agents in blue uniforms and face masks, with overhead signs warning against firearms. The audio, aligning with the lower third 'WASHINGTON POST: HUNDREDS OF PEOPLE BYPASSED PARTS OF AIRPORT SECURITY WITHIN LAST YEAR', reports that hundreds have bypassed airport security measures in the past 12 months by sneaking through ID checkpoints and going the wrong direction through one-way exit lanes, with a TSA spokesperson stating these are mostly inadvertent. Finally, the video shows the exterior of a '99¢ Only Stores' with a red awning, followed by interior shots of customers at checkout and a 'FARM FRESH IDEAS' delivery truck. The audio confirms, matching the lower third '99 CENT ONLY STORES SHUTTER ALL LOCATIONS', that 99 Cent Only Stores are shutting down all 371 locations nationwide due to factors like the pandemic and shifting consumer demand, with the chain set to liquidate all merchandise.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with dynamic clips of Bronny James, wearing USC jerseys with the number 6, engaged in basketball gameplay, while a lower third graphic announces 'The Five Things #5 BRONNY JAMES TO ENTER NBA DRAFT, TRANSFER PORTAL'. A male news anchor's voice immediately confirms that "USC freshman Bronny James, LeBron James's son, will enter the 2024 NBA draft, as well as the college transfer portal," adding a crucial detail that "over the summer, Bronny suffered a cardiac arrest while at a basketball workout" requiring a heart procedure that sidelined him for several months. The visual then shifts to the male news anchor himself, seated in a modern studio with a large screen behind him displaying 'Hallie Jackson NOW_', as he transitions to upcoming stories, stating, "Coming up from us, a deadly truck crash in Bolivia, what we're learning now about the driver, he may have fallen asleep at the wheel." This is followed by a 'COMING UP' preview showing the truck crash with emergency personnel, and then clips of female basketball players in Iowa and black and yellow uniforms, while the anchor continues, "And college athletes are navigating confusing new rules about how they can get paid, the one school trying to make that a lot easier, stay with us." After a full-screen 'Hallie Jackson NOW_' graphic, the studio environment reappears, now with the large screen displaying 'the global' and a collage of images, including a man with a framed certificate, a snowy landscape, and a person eating. The anchor introduces this segment, explaining that "NBC News covers hundreds of international stories every day, and because it's tough to read, watch, or listen to them all, our teams around the world have done it for both you and for me. Here's a look at what they're watching. We call the segment 'the global'." Throughout, the NBC News Now logo remains consistently visible in the lower right corner.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a news report on a tragic crash in Bolivia, showing emergency personnel in high-visibility vests at the scene of a collision between a bus and a truck carrying salt, with debris scattered across a mountainous road. On-screen text from 'Bolivian Govt TV' and 'NBC News Now' confirms at least 14 people are dead and two injured, with the audio detailing that local police arrested the truck driver, one of the only survivors, who they believe may have fallen asleep at the wheel. The scene transitions to Russia, where emergency responders in yellow and green uniforms marked 'МЧС РОССИИ' are seen evacuating people from flooded areas in the Ural Mountains, following a dam burst that forced a big evacuation, with something like 4,000 homes potentially inundated by muddy floodwaters caused by melting snow, as footage from the 'Governor of Orenburg Region Telegram Channel' shows submerged structures. The report then shifts to the UK, where a heartwarming moment unfolds in a cozy home: a representative from Guinness World Records presents a framed certificate to 111-year-old John Tinniswood, who is seated in a recliner, smiling in his glasses and plaid shirt. The audio identifies him as the world's oldest living man, born in 1912 before the First World War, who attributes his long life to "just luck," having earned the title after the previous 114-year-old record holder from Venezuela died earlier in the week. A caregiver is later seen serving John a meal. The video then cuts to a studio environment with a large blue screen displaying upcoming topics like 'ECLIPSE', 'HOSTAGE', 'DIDDY', 'BASKETBALL', and 'TAIWAN', before transitioning to a black-and-white press conference scene labeled 'MOTHER'S FIGHT', showing a woman speaking at a podium with a man beside her and photos of a young man on the table, introduced by the audio as "new reporting from NBC News."
segments.visual_original_caption: The video segment begins with a news report on a tragic crash in Bolivia, showing emergency personnel in high-visibility vests at the scene of a collision between a truck and a van, with debris scattered across the road and mountains in the background. The on-screen text from 'Bolivian Govt TV' and 'NBC News Now' confirms the incident resulted in at least 14 deaths. The scene transitions to Russia, where emergency responders in yellow and green uniforms with 'МЧС РОССИИ' (EMERCOM of Russia) markings are seen evacuating people from flooded areas following a dam burst in the Ural Mountains, with footage showing muddy floodwaters and submerged structures. The segment then shifts to a heartwarming moment in a cozy home setting, where a representative from Guinness World Records presents a framed certificate to 111-year-old John Tinniswood, who is seated in a recliner, smiling and wearing glasses and a plaid shirt; a caregiver later serves him a meal. The video then cuts to a studio environment with a large blue screen displaying a list of upcoming topics including 'ECLIPSE', 'HOSTAGE', 'DIDDY', 'BASKETBALL', and 'TAIWAN', before transitioning to a black-and-white press conference scene labeled 'MOTHER'S FIGHT', showing a woman speaking at a podium with a man beside her, with photos of a young man displayed on the table, indicating a story about a hostage situation in Gaza.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a large screen in a news studio displaying a black-and-white image labeled 'MOTHER'S FIGHT,' showing a woman speaking at a podium with a man beside her and photos of a young man on the table, set against a UN Security Council backdrop, as a male voice introduces the story of "one mother's fight to bring her son back home after he was taken hostage by Hamas back on October 7th." The scene transitions to a blue screen with the NBC News peacock logo, then cuts to personal photos of a smiling young man with a beard, identified by the male voice as "23-year-old Hersh Goldberg-Polin," shown in various settings including on a boat and in a living room with disco lights. The male voice continues, explaining that "in the nearly six months since her son was taken, Rachel Goldberg-Polin has become a vocal advocate in the effort to free the more than 100 hostages still being held by Hamas, all while trying to pretend everything is normal." The narrative shifts to Rachel Goldberg-Polin, identified by a lower third as the mother of hostage Hersh Goldberg-Polin, speaking emotionally in different indoor settings, her voice strained and emotional as she states, "Today is day 27. Hersh is still not home." She is seen wearing a gray t-shirt with a number pinned to it—first '163,' then '27,' and later '100'—symbolizing the days since her son was taken, which she recounts, "52, 75, 86. Today is day 100 since my son was stolen from me." Emotional background music plays as she reflects, "It's sad to be known for something really painful and really scary. And that's what people think of when they see me." She describes how media would ask, "How many days has it been?" The video shows her writing numbers on tape, taping them to her shirt, and sitting at a desk with dual monitors, emphasizing her ongoing, daily struggle. The lower third consistently reads 'MOTHER OF HOSTAGE TAKEN BY HAMAS FIGHTS FOR HER SON'S RETURN HOME' under the 'ISRAEL-HAMAS WAR' tag, with the NBC News Now logo in the corner. The segment concludes with a black screen displaying 'NBC NEWS DIGITAL DOCS' and the title 'STOLEN SON: A HOSTAGE'S MOM FIGHTS TO BRING HER CHILD HOME FROM GAZA,' reinforcing the documentary’s focus.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of a hand, later identified as Rachel Goldberg-Polin's, writing the number '163' on a small white piece of paper with a black marker, then taping it to her gray t-shirt. Rachel's voice explains, "masking tape and started to make these numbers. And it makes people really ask, how are we allowing this to continue to happen?" as she is shown sitting at a desk in an office, carefully placing the numbered tag on her chest amidst dual monitors, scissors, tape, and stacks of papers. A somber, reflective instrumental score plays throughout. The title card 'STOLEN SON: A HOSTAGE'S MOM FIGHTS TO BRING HER CHILD HOME FROM GAZA' appears, setting the documentary’s tone. The narrative shifts to Rachel walking hand-in-hand with a man through a cobblestone European square, likely Vatican City, as she speaks emotionally. Archival photos of her 23-year-old son, Hersh, are shown—smiling, making a rock gesture, and dancing with friends—accompanied by text explaining he was at the Supernova music festival when Hamas militants attacked on October 7, 2023, losing his left arm below the elbow and being taken hostage to Gaza. Rachel's voice continues, stating that "hostage families have to pretend to be normal. If we want to function and if we want to save them." She is then shown in a dimly lit office, flipping through a book, her face etched with sorrow, before returning to the camera, visibly distressed, as she recounts her fight for her son’s return. Her voice concludes, "First thing in the morning, I always say to myself, and now pretend to be human. And I get out of bed, and I put on this costume of being a person." The lower third consistently displays 'MOTHER OF HOSTAGE TAKEN BY HAMAS FIGHTS FOR HER SON'S RETURN HOME' under the 'ISRAEL-HAMAS WAR' tag, with the NBC News Now logo in the corner.

Her voice is reflective and determined, explaining her process of making numbered tags to prompt questions, describing the burden on hostage families to 'pretend to be normal' to function and save their loved ones, and revealing her daily struggle to 'pretend to be human' by putting on a 'costume' each morning.

He is identified as Rachel's 23-year-old son, Hersh, who was at the Supernova music festival when Hamas attacked, lost his left arm, and was taken hostage to Gaza.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with Rachel Goldberg-Polin, her dark hair pulled back, wearing a gray t-shirt with a number pinned to it, visually changing from '163' to '27' to '100', symbolizing the days since her son was taken hostage. In a dimly lit, industrial-style room with visible bookshelves and dual monitors, she emotionally recounts, "I have a walk that I do now in the mornings that I've crafted so that I don't see anybody and I walk with my head down and I always wear sunglasses... it's kind of like someone's holding a branding iron on your back and you can't let anybody know." She is shown reading from a book and walking, her expression somber and determined. The lower third graphic consistently displays 'MOTHER OF HOSTAGE TAKEN BY HAMAS FIGHTS FOR HER SON'S RETURN HOME' under the 'ISRAEL-HAMAS WAR' tag, with the NBC News Now logo. The scene transitions to a press conference at the UN Security Council in New York City on October 24, 2023, where Rachel stands at a podium with a UN microphone, flanked by several individuals, including a woman in a black blazer and a man in a blue shirt, all wearing lanyards, against a backdrop featuring the UN Security Council logo. Here, she states, "I was asked early on if I would speak at the United Nations in New York. And so I did." An urgent, questioning voice then asks, "Why is no one crying out for these people to be allowed access to the Red Cross? Why is no one demanding just proof of life?" The video then cuts to an outdoor rally where Rachel, wearing sunglasses and a black top, speaks at a podium surrounded by American and Israeli flags with a crowd of supporters behind her, declaring, "We felt that the advocacy for the hostage situation was so important that we really never wanted to say no," followed by applause. The segment also briefly includes a split-screen showing a model of a cityscape and a woman in a green coat speaking to a man, and earlier branding for 'NBC News Digital Docs' with the title 'STOLEN SON: A HOSTAGE'S MOM FIGHTS TO BRING HER CHILD HOME FROM GAZA' is visible. The overall tone is urgent and emotional, emphasizing her relentless advocacy and the international attention her case has garnered.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with Rachel Goldberg-Polin, a woman with dark hair pulled back, wearing a gray t-shirt with a number '163' pinned to it, seated at a desk in a dimly lit room. Her expression is focused and somber as she works on a laptop, the camera zooming in on her typing hands before cutting to a close-up of her face. Speaking directly to the camera with an emotional, somber voice, Rachel describes having 'episodes where I do break,' explaining how she tries to manage these moments by seeking privacy, but acknowledges 'that's not the way trauma works,' leading to times where she's 'screaming or crying or laying on the street.' Throughout her testimony, a lower third graphic consistently reads 'MOTHER OF HOSTAGE TAKEN BY HAMAS FIGHTS FOR HER SON'S RETURN HOME' under the 'ISRAEL-HAMAS WAR' tag, with the NBC News Now logo. The scene then abruptly transitions to chaotic footage from Deir al-Balah, Gaza, showing civilians fleeing through rubble at night, accompanied by urgent, rhythmic chanting of 'Hey, ho!' and other crowd noises. An ambulance is visible, and a woman carries a child amidst the destruction. The intensity escalates as medical personnel in gloves attend to a severely injured infant lying on the ground, covered in dust and debris, while others rush to help, creating an atmosphere of desperation and humanitarian crisis. Rachel's voice returns, expressing profound sorrow: 'I'm also extremely heartbroken over the unbelievable suffering of hundreds of thousands of innocent Gazan people. What they are going through, it's unbearable.' The segment also features branding for 'NBC News Digital Docs' and the title 'STOLEN SON: A HOSTAGE'S MOM FIGHTS TO BRING HER CHILD HOME FROM GAZA' on a black screen.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with chaotic footage of civilians fleeing through rubble in Deir Al-Balah, Gaza, carrying belongings and children, amidst smoke and destruction, with a distressed infant being treated on the ground. A lower third graphic reads 'MOTHER OF HOSTAGE TAKEN BY HAMAS FIGHTS FOR HER SON'S RETURN HOME'. An emotional female voice, later identified as Rachel Goldberg-Polin, states, "So painful to watch. Right now, there are hundreds of thousands of people suffering in this, on both sides. And it's time for the suffering to stop." The scene shifts to a protest where people hold signs with photos of a young man, including 'BRING OMER BACK HOME' and 'BRING HIM HOME NOW!', accompanied by chanting and an Israeli flag. A close-up shows Rachel Goldberg-Polin, visibly emotional, wearing a gray t-shirt with a number pinned to it, changing from '163' to '27' to '100', symbolizing the days since her son was taken. She continues, "On Shabbat morning, I sit down cross-legged right in front of this big poster that we have of him that is behind our front door. I look into his eyes and I talk to him and I tell him, 'You have to stay strong mentally, spiritually, psychologically, physically, religiously. You have to survive. You have no choice. We are coming. You can make it through this and you're going to have a long beautiful life.'" During her speech, archival photos of her son, Omer, are shown: shirtless on a rocky cliff, smiling on a boat in a colorful shirt, dancing in a living room with disco lights, and in a family photo with his parents on a boat. The segment transitions to a news studio where a male anchor in a dark suit and light blue checkered shirt speaks, with a large screen behind him displaying 'Hallie Jackson NOW_'. The screen then cycles through a black-and-white image of Diddy and his son Christian Combs at the VMAs, labeled 'SON NOW ACCUSED', followed by a blue screen with the text 'THE FIVE THINGS' and a list of topics including 'HOSTAGE', 'DIDDY', 'BASKETBALL', and 'TAIWAN'. The anchor concludes, "One mother. We'll be right back."
