segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a large news studio screen displaying a black-and-white image of recording artist Sean 'Diddy' Combs and his 26-year-old son Christian Combs at the VMAs, accompanied by the text 'SON NOW ACCUSED,' as a news anchor introduces an NBC News exclusive about Diddy's son being accused of sexual assault. The scene transitions to a performance clip of Christian Combs on stage, smiling and holding a microphone, while a blue NBC News Exclusive banner states 'NEW LAWSUIT ACCUSES DIDDY'S SON CHRISTIAN OF SEXUALLY ASSAULTING WOMAN ON A YACHT,' specifically in December 2022. A close-up of legal documents then appears, highlighting a quote about 'DEFENDANT S. COMBS' turning a "wholesome family excursion" on a Diddy-chartered yacht into a "hedonistic environment," with the anchor adding that Diddy himself is named in the lawsuit for aiding and abetting his son. The video cuts to a split-screen showing Diddy and Christian Combs in red outfits at the VMAs, reinforcing their public image. A blue graphic then displays a statement from Aaron Dyer, identified by the anchor as a lawyer for Diddy and his son, dismissing the lawsuit as 'lewd and meritless' and announcing a motion to dismiss. The segment continues with a three-panel split screen showing aerial footage of Diddy’s Los Angeles mansion, a performance clip of Diddy on stage, and a van in Miami, all dated March 25, with the anchor recalling dramatic police raids at Diddy's homes earlier this month. The video concludes with a close-up of Diddy performing on stage, wearing sunglasses and a black-and-white palm tree shirt, with the lawsuit banner persisting, as the anchor states Diddy has denied all allegations related to sexual assault and sex trafficking. Throughout, a serious news-style background music plays.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a split-screen view featuring a male news anchor in a dark suit and light blue checkered shirt, alongside Hallie Jackson, who wears a light pink blazer over a ruffled blouse with a pearl necklace, both seated in a modern news studio. A lower third graphic displays 'NBC NEWS EXCLUSIVE' and the headline 'NEW LAWSUIT ACCUSES DIDDY'S SON CHRISTIAN OF SEXUALLY ASSAULTING WOMAN ON A YACHT'. The male anchor then asks Chloe Melas, "Chloe, what else can you tell us about this lawsuit against Christian and Diddy Combs, and did this come as a surprise?" The scene transitions to a full-screen view of Chloe Melas, identified as the NBC News Entertainment Correspondent, speaking directly to the camera with a composed demeanor, against a background featuring a large blue screen with 'Hallie Jackson NOW_' and multiple monitors. Chloe responds, detailing that "this woman is accusing Christian Combs of an incident that allegedly took place in December 2022, just days before Diddy had a really big party on this yacht for New Year's Eve." She explains that "the victim claims that she was drugged and sexually assaulted by Christian Combs," noting that "Christian Combs's lawyer denying these allegations." Chloe adds that the lawsuit claims "there are also audio recordings that were taken by a music producer... who was always recording uh, things that were going on and just happened to catch some of the exchanges between Christian Combs and this alleged victim that they believe backs up her claims." She clarifies that "NBC News has not been able to independently verify that," and mentions that "in the lawsuit, there are also uh, photos of alleged bruises that she showed." As Chloe speaks, the visual cuts to a full-screen graphic titled 'NBC News Exclusive: Son of Sean 'Diddy' Combs Accused of Sexual Assault_', which lists detailed allegations including drugging and sexually assaulting a victim on a yacht, Christian Combs arriving 'heavily intoxicated,' giving inappropriate attention, and allegedly groping the victim. The graphic also accuses Sean 'Diddy' Combs of premises liability for chartering the yacht and allegedly aiding and abetting his son, featuring photos of Christian Combs in a light-colored shirt and chain, and Sean 'Diddy' Combs in a white suit with a gold chain. The NBC News Now logo is visible in the lower right corner throughout the graphic.

The scene transitions to a full-screen view of Chloe Melas, identified as the NBC News Entertainment Correspondent, speaking directly to the camera with a composed demeanor. The background features a large blue screen with 'Hallie Jackson NOW_' and multiple monitors displaying the same text. The segment then cuts to a full-screen graphic titled 'NBC News Exclusive: Son of Sean 'Diddy' Combs Accused of Sexual Assault_', listing detailed allegations against Christian Combs and Sean 'Diddy' Combs. The graphic is flanked by photos of Christian Combs on the left, wearing a light-colored shirt and a chain necklace, and Sean 'Diddy' Combs on the right, in a white suit with a gold chain. The allegations include drugging and sexually assaulting a victim on a yacht in December 2022, arriving 'heavily intoxicated,' giving inappropriate attention, and allegedly groping the victim. Sean 'Diddy' Combs is accused of premises liability for chartering the yacht and allegedly aiding and abetting his son. The graphic remains on screen for the remainder of the segment, with the NBC News Now logo visible in the lower right corner.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of a woman’s forearm, revealing a visible bruise circled in red near a small black tattoo, presented as evidence in the lawsuit 'GRACE O'MARCAIGH V. CHRISTIAN COMBS'. An 'NBC NEWS EXCLUSIVE' banner highlights the headline: 'NEW LAWSUIT ACCUSES DIDDY'S SON CHRISTIAN OF SEXUALLY ASSAULTING WOMAN ON A YACHT'. The scene then transitions to Hallie Jackson, seated in a modern news studio with a blue backdrop, wearing a light pink blazer and pearl necklace, speaking directly to the camera. She explains that Christian Combs allegedly grabbed the woman, and the incident took place in December 2022, with the woman seeking unspecified financial damages. Hallie notes that Sean 'Diddy' Combs and his son are both fighting the allegations, with Diddy included because he procured the yacht. A male news anchor, in a dark suit, joins Hallie in a split-screen, asking her to elaborate on the allegations against Diddy and the investigation's status. The lower third updates to 'NEW LAWSUIT ACCUSES DIDDY OF AIDING AND ABETTING HIS SON IN SEX ASSAULT ON A YACHT'. Hallie then proceeds to detail a timeline, explaining that the lawsuits "kicked off" in November 2023 when Diddy's ex-girlfriend, Cassandra Ventura, a well-known singer, and two others sued Diddy for sexual abuse. This explanation coincides with a full-screen graphic titled 'Timeline of Lawsuits Against Sean 'Diddy' Combs_', displaying five labeled boxes from November 2023 to April 2024, alongside a photo of Diddy. The NBC News Now logo remains visible throughout the segment.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a dynamic transition featuring a 'COMING UP' banner and a golden NCAA logo, leading into a modern news studio. A male news anchor, dressed in a dark suit and light blue checkered shirt, is seated at a sleek red desk, mostly off-screen, speaking in a clear, professional voice about the NCAA Women's Final Four. Behind him, a large blue screen displays upcoming topics like 'BASKETBALL' and 'TAIWAN', while a side screen shows 'Hallie Jackson NOW_'. The anchor highlights the high ticket prices for the Final Four, quoting the NCAA president on the sport's success. The main screen then transitions to a black-and-white NCAA logo under 'WOMEN'S FINAL FOUR', followed by a bracket graphic detailing the matchups: South Carolina vs. NC State and Iowa vs. UConn, complete with game times. As the anchor discusses the 'four huge storylines' and specific players like UConn's Paige Bueckers and Iowa's Caitlin Clark, action clips of UConn and Iowa players (UCONN 5, IOWA 24) are shown. The visual then shifts to a graphic titled 'Iowa-LSU Shatters Women's Basketball Viewership Record', noting 12.3 million viewers for the 2024 Elite Eight game, surpassing the previous record of 9.9 million. Finally, a 'Women's NCAA Tournament Final Could Break Another Record' prediction graphic appears, forecasting potential viewership for various final matchups, ranging from 8 million to nearly 17 million, attributed to 'The Athletic's Richard Deitsch'. The entire segment maintains a professional, data-driven atmosphere with blue lighting and an NBC peacock logo on the desk base, while the NBC News Now Logo is consistently displayed in the lower right corner.
segments.visual_original_caption: The video begins with a dynamic transition into a news segment focused on the NCAA Women's March Madness Final Four, highlighted by a bold 'COMING UP' banner and a golden NCAA logo. The scene shifts to a modern news studio with a large blue screen displaying upcoming topics like 'BASKETBALL' and 'TAIWAN', while a side screen shows 'Hallie Jackson NOW_'. The studio features a sleek red desk where a male news anchor, dressed in a dark suit and light blue checkered shirt, is seated, though he remains off-screen for most of the segment. The main screen transitions through various graphics: first, a black-and-white NCAA logo under 'WOMEN'S FINAL FOUR', then a bracket showing the four teams — South Carolina, Iowa, NC State, and UConn — with game times. This is followed by action clips of UConn and Iowa players in game, including a close-up of a UConn player in a black jersey with 'UCONN 5' and an Iowa player in a yellow jersey with 'IOWA 24'. The screen then displays a graphic titled 'Iowa-LSU Shatters Women's Basketball Viewership Record', noting 12.3 million viewers for the 2024 Elite Eight game, surpassing the previous record of 9.9 million. Finally, a prediction graphic titled 'Women's NCAA Tournament Final Could Break Another Record' shows expert forecasts: Iowa vs. South Carolina at 16.9 million, Iowa vs. UConn at 14.1 million, UConn vs. South Carolina at 10 million, and UConn vs. NC State at 8 million, attributed to 'The Athletic's Richard Deitsch'. The studio environment is sleek and modern, with blue lighting and a large NBC peacock logo on the desk base, maintaining a professional and data-driven atmosphere throughout.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a split-screen view in a news studio, showing two men within a large framed display. On the left, NBC News Correspondent Jesse Kirsch stands outdoors in Cleveland, in front of a glass building adorned with '2024 Women's Final Four Cleveland' banners and team logos, wearing a navy blue jacket over a light blue shirt. On the right, a man in a dark suit is seated in a studio with monitors. The scene transitions to a full split-screen with a Male News Anchor in a dark suit and light blue checkered shirt, seated in the studio with 'Hallie Jackson NOW_' behind him. The lower third displays 'MARCH MADNESS_' and 'TONIGHT: WOMEN'S FINAL FOUR TIPS OFF AS EXPERTS PREDICT NEW TV RECORDS'. The Male News Anchor, speaking about the final possibly being Iowa against South Carolina and how NBC Sam Brock will discuss athlete earnings, then introduces Jesse Kirsch, playfully remarking, "Boy, you got a rough assignment, buddy. You get you get to watch the Final Four." Jesse Kirsch smiles and nods in response. The video cuts to a full-screen view of Jesse Kirsch in Cleveland, speaking animatedly with hand gestures. He responds, "Yeah, I have a press pass that I think would be worth a pretty penny, but of course, ethics preclude me from indulging in that." He continues, explaining they will check out the games, setting the stage for "powerhouse matchups" and "the finale to what has been a historic tournament for the women's game." He highlights that the NCAA reports broken attendance records for each round leading up to this weekend, specifically mentioning the LSU-Iowa rematch from last year's championship, which drew more viewers than the previous year's final, emphasizing the "energy around these games." Pedestrians walk and vehicles pass in the background, with the '2024 Women's Final Four Cleveland' banner prominently displayed under natural daylight, reflecting the lively, anticipatory atmosphere.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with NBC News Correspondent Jesse Kirsch, speaking live from Cleveland in front of a glass building adorned with '2024 Women's Final Four Cleveland' banners and team logos. Wearing a navy blue jacket over a light blue shirt, Jesse gestures as he reports on the upcoming Women's Final Four, with a lower third graphic indicating 'TONIGHT: WOMEN'S FINAL FOUR TIPS OFF AS EXPERTS PREDICT NEW TV RECORDS'. He highlights the high demand, stating that ticket prices are "almost double what they were last year." This leads into a blue graphic titled 'Most Expensive Women's Final Four Ever_', displaying stark ticket price data: an average purchase price of $572 (96% more than last year's $291) and a 'Get-in' price of $451 (401% more than last year's $90), sourced from TickPic. The video then cuts back to Jesse, with a lower third confirming 'NEW: TICKET PRICES NEARLY 100% MORE THAN LAST YEAR'S WOMEN'S FINAL FOUR'. The scene shifts to an indoor basketball court where Jesse interviews Lynn Holzman, NCAA VP of Women's Basketball, and Charlie Baker, NCAA President. Lynn, in a navy blazer over a light blue top, gestures while explaining that the rise is a "combination of both uh, you know, exposure through TV, but it's social media," adding that "the skill and athleticism on the court is phenomenal." The lower third identifies Lynn and states 'NCAA WOMEN'S BASKETBALL VP: COMBINATION OF TV EXPOSURE, SOCIAL MEDIA HAS FUELED RISE'. The segment returns to Jesse Kirsch in Cleveland, who then transitions to a split-screen with a Male News Anchor (Tom) in the Studio Environment. Tom holds up a phone and speaks, joking about Jesse's press pass, while Jesse continues reporting, mentioning "Caitlin Clark" as a standout name. The studio backdrop displays 'Hallie Jackson NOW_' and the 'MARCH MADNESS_' tag remains visible throughout.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a split-screen, where a Male News Anchor in a dark suit and light blue checkered shirt, seated in a studio with a 'Hallie Jackson NOW_' and 'MARCH MADNESS_' screen behind him, speaks about the disparity between women's basketball playing in typical 18,000-seat arenas versus men's in 70,000-seat football stadiums, questioning when parity might be approached. On the right, NBC News Correspondent Jesse Kirsch, wearing a navy blue jacket, smiles and speaks outdoors in Cleveland in front of a glass building adorned with '2024 Women's Final Four Cleveland' banners, with a lower third noting 'NCAA WOMEN'S BASKETBALL VP: COMBINATION OF TV EXPOSURE, SOCIAL MEDIA HAS FUELED RISE'. The scene transitions to a full-screen view of Jesse Kirsch, gesturing animatedly with 'CLEVELAND' and 'TONIGHT: WOMEN'S FINAL FOUR TIPS OFF AS EXPERTS PREDICT NEW TV RECORDS' on screen. He explains that earlier discussions with the NCAA addressed past disparities, like weight room facilities in the 2021 tournament, and highlights the NCAA President and VP's commitment to investment for parity in both men's and women's tournaments, noting that even with future tournaments planned, they are looking at larger venues for women, expecting continued growth. The video then cuts to an indoor basketball court marked with 'NCAA WOMEN'S' branding and surrounded by empty red seats, where Jesse Kirsch interviews Lynn Holzman, NCAA VP of Women's Basketball (in a navy blazer), and Charlie Baker, NCAA President (in a dark suit). Charlie Baker, identified by a lower third, gestures as he states that women's basketball's future is 'something where there is no peak,' and it's 'just going to keep going up.'

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens on a basketball court during the 2024 NCAA Women's Tournament, where Jesse Kirsch, in a dark suit, interviews NCAA President Charlie Baker and NCAA VP of Women's Basketball Lynn Holzman. Baker, also in a dark suit, gestures expressively as he speaks about the importance of continued investments and adaptability for the sport's future, stating, "for all of us is to continue to make the investments to drive that, but at the same time, to be able to make the changes despite the fact that we have to plan many years in advance, to be able to absorb and take advantage of that." Holzman stands with her hands clasped, while the court features orange and black lines and is surrounded by empty red seats. A lower third graphic displays 'MARCH MADNESS_' and the headline 'NCAA PRESIDENT: I SEE WOMEN'S BASKETBALL'S FUTURE AS SOMETHING WHERE THERE IS NO PEAK'. The scene then cuts to Jesse Kirsch, now wearing a navy blue jacket over a light blue shirt, reporting live from outside a glass building in Cleveland. He gestures as he speaks, highlighting the sport's growing popularity, "And that's of course what we're talking about in- in-person attendance specifically, but don't forget, Tom, as you mentioned, talking about more than 12 million people that tuned in on Monday night for a quarterfinal. ESPN said that was the most watched women's college basketball game ever." Behind him, large circular banners read '2024 Women's Final Four Cleveland' with team logos, and people walk in the background. The final scene shifts to a bright, professional news studio where Lester Holt, in a dark pinstripe suit and glasses, sits at a desk with an NBC-branded white mug. He addresses the viewers, saying, "Thanks for watching. Stay updated about breaking news and top stories on the NBC News app or follow us on social media." The backdrop displays 'NBC NIGHTLY NEWS with LESTER HOLT' in bold white text against a blue and purple abstract design.
